title: What's Wrong With The Church
subtitle: Is It Fixable
author: Ashley Evans
description: A bold look at what's broken in the modern church — and a passionate call to rediscover God's original design for His bride.

chapters.title: Introduction: The Download
paragraphs:
The Download The Download
Let me tell you about the moment everything changed for me. I had an encounter with Jesus that I can only describe as overwhelming. It was like a download of 4 trillion gigabytes of information all at
once. In that moment, I understood things I'd never understood before. I saw
things I'd never seen before. I received things I'd never received
before. But here's what's important: In that download, He didn't just
download His love for me.

He downloaded His love for the church. I didn't just encounter Jesus and say, "Wow, I love Jesus!"
I encountered Jesus and said, "Wow, I love Jesus---and I love what He
loves."
It wasn't optional. It wasn't separate. It was part of the
download. And that moment changed the trajectory of my life. Before that encounter, I attended church. I believed church was
important. I knew the theology. But I didn't have the revelation.

After that encounter? Everything shifted. I didn't just attend church---I loved the church. I didn't just believe it was important---I saw it as the most important
thing on earth. I didn't just participate---I gave my life to it. Because I had seen what Jesus is building. WHY I WROTE THIS BOOK
I wrote this book because I'm tired of hearing Christians say, "I love
Jesus, but I don't love the church."
I'm tired of watching people disconnect from the body of Christ and
wonder why their faith is dying.

I'm tired of seeing the bride of Christ criticized, abandoned, and
rejected by the very people Jesus died to save. And I'm convinced that the problem isn't the church. The problem is
that most Christians haven't seen what I've seen. They haven't had the revelation. They don't yet understand that Jesus and His church are one. So I wrote this book to help you see what I've seen. Not to guilt you. Not to shame you.

Not to make you feel bad about your
past. But to open your eyes to something you can't unsee. Because once you see it---once you truly understand what the church is
and why Jesus gave His life to create it---everything changes. You stop making excuses. You stop criticizing from the outside. You stop trying to separate what God has joined together. And you fall in love with the bride. WHAT THIS BOOK IS (AND ISN'T)
Let me be clear about what this book is and what it isn't.

This book is NOT:
•  A defense of church hurt or bad leadership
•  An argument that all churches are perfect
•  A guilt trip designed to make you attend church
•  A theological treatise on ecclesiology
•  An excuse for churches to avoid change and growth
This book IS:
•  A revelation of why Jesus and the church are inseparable
•  A challenge to stop believing the lie that you can love Jesus
without loving His bride
•  A vision of what God designed the church to be
•  An invitation to fall in love with what Jesus died to create
•  A call to commit to an imperfect church because Jesus is committed
to her
If you've been hurt by the church, I see you.

I've been hurt too. If you're frustrated with the church, I understand. I've been
frustrated too. If you're wondering if the church is even worth it, I get it. I've
been there too. But I'm writing this book to tell you: Yes, it's worth it. Not because the church is perfect. But because Jesus loves her. Not because Christians are flawless. But because God is building
something eternal. Not because it's easy. But because it's what Jesus died for.

HOW TO READ THIS BOOK
This book is structured in six parts, each building on the previous one:
Part One (Chapters 1-3) breaks down the lie that you can love Jesus
without loving the church. If you've ever said, "I love Jesus but not
the church," start here. Part Two (Chapters 4-6) lays the foundation for what the church
really is---the body of Christ, the mystery revealed, the gathering of
believers.

This is where theology meets reality. Part Three (Chapters 7-10) shows you why the church is the most
important organization on planet earth. You'll see why Jesus gave all
authority for the church and why God has no Plan B. Part Four (Chapters 11-13) reveals the power that's released when
believers gather. You'll discover what you can do together that you
could never do alone. Part Five (Chapters 14-16) addresses the hard stuff---how to love an
imperfect church, how to stay committed when people disappoint you, and
why you need revelation to see what Jesus is building.

Part Six (Chapters 17-18) shows you where to find Jesus and what
fullness looks like when you're connected to His body. My recommendation? Read it in order. Each chapter builds on the previous
one. But if you're in a hurry or need to address a specific issue, feel free
to jump to the chapter that speaks to where you are right now. MY PRAYER FOR YOU
Before you turn the page and start Chapter 1, let me pray for you:
Father, I pray for every person reading this book.

Open their eyes to see what they've never seen before. Give them a revelation of the church that changes everything. Soften their hearts toward Your bride, even if they've been hurt.
*Help them understand that Jesus and the church are one---and that they
can't love one without loving the other.*
*Break the lie that's kept them isolated, disconnected, and
incomplete.*
And bring them home to the family You created them to be part of.

In Jesus' name, Amen. Now let's begin. Let me show you why the church is the most important thing on earth. And why you can't love Jesus without loving His bride. Turn the page and let's start with Chapter 1: The Lie Everyone
Believes.

chapters.title: Chapter 1: The Lie Everyone Believes
paragraphs:
I need to tell you something that's going to offend you. And I'm okay with that. Because what I'm about to say might be the most important thing you
hear all year. Here it is:
If you say "I love Jesus but not the church," you're believing a
lie. Not just any lie. THE lie---the one the enemy has been using for decades
to keep Christians isolated, ineffective, and disconnected from the very
thing Jesus died to create.

I know some of you just bristled reading that. Your defenses went up. You've got a list of reasons why the church hurt you, disappointed you,
or drove you away. I get it. I've been there. But stick with me. Because I'm going to show you something that will
blow your mind. THE FACEBOOK POST THAT WENT VIRAL
A few years ago, I posted something on Facebook that created quite the
controversy. I wrote: "You can be a Christian without church like being 100 feet
underwater without breathing---for about 4 minutes."
Oh man, did that post blow up.

People were furious. "How dare you say that!" "That's so
judgmental!" "I have a personal relationship with Jesus and I don't
need the church!"
But here's the thing: I wasn't being provocative for the sake of
being provocative. I was telling the truth. And the truth is this: You can survive underwater without
breathing---for about 4 minutes. After that, you die. You can survive as a Christian without the church---for a season.

But
eventually, your faith suffocates. Your passion fades. Your connection
to Jesus weakens. Not because Jesus left you. But because you disconnected from His
body. Let me explain. THE CRISIS WE'RE IN
Here's what's happening right now in America:
According to recent studies, only 30% of Americans attend religious
services regularly. That's down from over 50% just a few decades ago. Even more alarming: After COVID-19, church attendance has still not
fully recovered.

Many churches are operating at only 85% of their
pre-pandemic levels. But here's what's even more disturbing:
It's not just that people stopped going to church. It's that they've
convinced themselves they don't need to. "I love Jesus, but I don't need the church." "I can worship
God anywhere." "The church is full of hypocrites." "I'm
spiritual, not religious."
And on the surface, these statements sound reasonable.

Even noble. But they're based on a fundamental misunderstanding of what the church
is and why Jesus created it. WHOM GOD HAS JOINED TOGETHER
Let me tell you about my wife, Jane. When we first got married, Jane had this habit that drove me crazy. She
would point to these tiny veins on her legs and say, "Look at these
varicose veins. Don't you think they're awful?"
Honestly? I hadn't even noticed them. To me, they were barely visible.

But she kept bringing them up. Kept pointing them out. Kept apologizing
for them. Finally, one day I sat her down and said something I meant with
everything in me:
"Jane, stop pointing out your blemishes. When I married you, I
married ALL of you. I don't love part of you and tolerate the other
parts. I love YOU---veins and all. And if you keep talking badly about
yourself, if you keep asking me to focus on what you think is wrong with
you, you might actually get me to dislike those parts.

But here's the
thing---if I stop loving any part of you, I won't love any part of you. Because you're not parts. You're one person. And I love all of
you."
That conversation changed something in our marriage. Now let me show you where I'm going with this. Matthew 19:6 -- *"So they are no longer two, but one flesh. Therefore what God has joined together, let no one separate."*
Jesus said those words about marriage.

But here's what most people
miss:
Jesus applies the same principle to Himself and the church. Ephesians 5:31-32 -- *"For this reason a man will leave his father
and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh. This is a profound mystery---but I am talking about Christ and the
church."*
Read that again. Paul quotes the Genesis passage about marriage. And then he says,
"I'm talking about Christ and the church."
Do you see it?

Jesus and the church are one. Not metaphorically. Not symbolically. They are ONE. Just like a husband and wife become one flesh, Jesus and the church are
inseparably joined. And what God has joined together, let no man separate. YOU CAN'T LOVE ONE WITHOUT LOVING THE OTHER
Here's where this gets personal:
If Jesus and the church are one, then you cannot love Jesus while
rejecting the church. It's impossible.

It would be like me saying to Jane, "I love you, but I hate your hands. I love you, but I can't stand your voice. I love you, but your
personality drives me crazy."
That's not love. That's rejection. And when you say, "I love Jesus but not the church," you're doing the
same thing. You're trying to separate what God has joined together. You're saying, "I'll take Jesus, but I don't want His bride."
And Jesus looks at you and says, "She's part of Me.

If you reject
her, you reject Me."
THE GREATEST HOAX EVER PERPETRATED
Let me be blunt:
The idea that you can love Jesus without loving the church is the
greatest hoax ever perpetrated on Christians. It's a lie straight from the pit of hell, designed to keep you
isolated, disconnected, and ineffective. Because here's what the enemy knows:
An isolated Christian is a weak Christian. When you disconnect from the body of Christ:
•  You lose accountability
•  You lose community
•  You lose perspective
•  You lose the power that comes from unity
•  You lose your spiritual sharpness
And over time, your faith withers.

Not because God abandoned you. But because you abandoned the very thing
God designed to sustain you. BUT THE CHURCH HURT ME
I know what some of you are thinking:
"But Ashley, you don't understand. The church hurt me. Christians
disappointed me. Leaders failed me."
I get it. I really do. I've been hurt by the church too. I've been disappointed by
Christians. I've seen leaders fall. But here's what I need you to understand:
The church isn't perfect because it's made up of imperfect people.

Including you. Including me. We're all broken. We're all flawed. We're all works in progress. And yes, sometimes the church gets it wrong. Sometimes Christians fail. Sometimes leaders fall. But that doesn't change what Jesus said:
The church is His bride. And He's coming back for her. Not a perfect bride. A redeemed bride. A bride He's washing, preparing,
and making holy. And if Jesus is committed to her, you should be too.

Think of it like
this. Planes crash every day all over the world. Most of them are small
planes but big planes go down too. And here's the truth, no one stops
believing in flying because 1 plane or 20 planes crash, When families
fail and they often do, normal people don't stop believing in family and
it is the same with the church. It's foolish to stop believing in the
church because 1 leader or even 20 failed.

THE INVITATION
So here's what I'm asking you to do:
Stop believing the lie. Stop saying, "I love Jesus but not the church."
Stop trying to separate what God has joined together. And start seeing the church the way Jesus sees her. Not as a building. Not as an institution. Not as a group of hypocrites. But as His bride. His body. His beloved. The thing He gave His life to create. The thing He's coming back for.

The thing He's building right now, brick by brick, person by person. And you're part of it. Whether you like it or not. Whether you've been attending or avoiding. You're part of the body of Christ. So stop running from it. And start running toward it. Because in the next seventeen chapters, I'm going to show you something
you can't unsee. I'm going to show you why Jesus and the church are one. Why you can't have Jesus without His bride.

Why the church is the most important thing on planet earth. And why your life will never be complete until you're connected to His
body. Are you ready? Let's go. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about the half-empty tomb. Because most Christians celebrate the resurrection---but they miss what
Jesus did immediately after He rose from the dead. And it changes
everything.*

chapters.title: Chapter 2: The Half-Empty Tomb
paragraphs:
Every Easter, churches around the world celebrate the same thing:
Jesus died for our sins. And that's absolutely true. It's the gospel. It's the foundation of
our faith.
1 Corinthians 15:3-4 -- *For what I received I passed on to you as
of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the
Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day
according to the Scriptures.*
Christ died for our sins.

We sing about it. We preach about it. We thank God for it. But here's what most Christians never realize:
That's only half of what Jesus accomplished on the cross. Jesus didn't just die to save you from sin. He died to create the church. And if you miss that second purpose, you'll miss the entire point of
the gospel. THE TWO PURPOSES OF THE CROSS
Let me show you something in Scripture that most people skip right over:
Ephesians 2:14-16 -- *For he himself is our peace, who has made the
two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of
hostility, by setting aside in his flesh the law with its commands and
regulations.

His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity out
of the two, thus making peace, and in one body to reconcile both of them
to God through the cross, by which he put to death their hostility.*
Read that slowly. His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity. To
reconcile both of them to God through the cross. Do you see it? The cross had two purposes:
1. To reconcile individuals to God (that's what we celebrate on
Easter)
2.

To create one new humanity---the church (that's what we almost
never talk about)
Ephesians 5:25 -- *Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved
the church and gave himself up for her.*
Christ gave himself up for her. Not just for you. Not just for me. For the church. Jesus didn't die just to forgive your sin. He died to create a bride. A
family. A body. A people who would carry His name and His mission into
the world.

And most of us only celebrate half of what He accomplished. WHY THIS MATTERS
You might be thinking, "Okay, that's interesting. But why does it
matter? I'm saved. I'm going to heaven. Isn't that enough?"
Here's why it matters:
If you only understand the first purpose of the cross, you'll live a
self-centered Christian life. You'll see Jesus as your personal Savior. Your ticket to heaven. Your
get-out-of-hell-free card.

And your faith will revolve around you:
•  What Jesus can do for you
•  How Jesus makes you feel
•  What blessings you can receive
But when you understand the second purpose of the cross---that Jesus
died to create the church---everything changes. Suddenly, it's not just about you. It's about us. It's about the body. It's about the mission. It's about what Jesus is building on the earth. And you realize that being saved isn't the finish line---it's the
starting line.

THE PICTURE OF THE TOMB
Think about the empty tomb on resurrection morning. When Mary Magdalene and the other women arrived at the tomb, they found
it empty. The stone was rolled away. Jesus was gone. Luke 24:5-6 -- *"Why do you look for the living among the dead? He
is not here; he has risen!"*
We celebrate the empty tomb. We sing "He is risen!" We rejoice that
death couldn't hold Him. But here's what I want you to see:
The tomb wasn't just empty.

It was half-empty. What do I mean by that? The tomb was empty of Jesus. But it was full of something else:
purpose. Jesus didn't rise from the dead just to go back to heaven and leave us
alone. He rose from the dead to build His church. Matthew 16:18 -- *"And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this
rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome
it."*
Notice the tense: "I WILL build."
Not "I might build." Not "If you're good enough, I'll build."
"I WILL build."
It's a promise.

A declaration. A certainty. Jesus rose from the dead with a mission: to build something that would
last forever, outlast every kingdom on earth, and storm the gates of
hell itself. And that something is the church. THE DOUBLE CELEBRATION
So here's what I want you to do this Easter---and every day after:
Celebrate both purposes of the cross. Yes, celebrate that Jesus died for your sins. That He took your place.

That He gave you forgiveness, redemption, and eternal life. But also celebrate that He died to create the church. That He gave His life to build something bigger than you. That He rose from the dead to unite Jew and Gentile, slave and free,
male and female into one body. That He's still building His church today---and you're part of it. When you understand both purposes, everything changes:
•  Your faith stops being self-centered and starts being
mission-centered
•  Your relationship with Jesus includes your relationship with His
body
•  Your worship becomes about what He's building, not just what He's
done for you
•  Your commitment to the church becomes non-negotiable
YOU'RE PART OF SOMETHING BIGGER
Here's the most important thing I want you to understand:
You're not the main character in this story.

I know that sounds harsh. But it's true. The gospel isn't primarily about you getting saved so you can go to
heaven. The gospel is about God building a kingdom---and you're invited to be
part of it. Colossians 1:13 -- *For he has rescued us from the dominion of
darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves.*
You were rescued into something. Not just rescued from something. You were brought into a kingdom.

A family. A body. A mission. And that spiritual kingdom until Jesus returns is represented by the
Church. Jesus mission station, his hands and feet on the earth. The only
visible representation of Jesus on the earth until he returns. So stop reducing the cross to a personal transaction. Stop thinking of Jesus as your personal genie who exists to make your
life better. Stop celebrating only half of what He accomplished.

And start celebrating that you're part of what He died to create. THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge for you in this chapter:
Stop reducing the cross to a personal transaction. Yes, Jesus died for you. But He also died for something much bigger than
you. He died to create a people. A family. A body. A bride. And you're part of that. Not because you're special. But because He's gracious. Not because you deserve it.

But because He chose you. Jesus didn't die just to save you from hell. He died to bring you into
the church. And when you see that---when you really see that---you can't help but
fall in love with the thing He gave His life to create. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about the bride Jesus is
coming back for. And here's the shocking truth: it's not you. At
least, not you individually. You're invited to the wedding.

But you're
not the bride by yourself. Let me show you what that means and why it
matters more than you think.*

chapters.title: Chapter 3: The Bride Jesus Is Coming Back For
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about the most anticipated wedding in history. There's a groom who's been preparing for this day since before time
began. He's built a home. He's made every arrangement. He's paid the
ultimate price for His bride. And now He's waiting for the day when He
can finally return and take her home. The invitation has been sent out to billions of people across thousands
of years. The bride is getting ready.

And one day---maybe sooner than we think---the groom is coming back. But here's what most people miss: You're invited to the wedding. But
you're not the bride. At least, not by yourself. Let me explain. THE GREATEST LOVE STORY EVER TOLD
Every great story has a romance at its center. Romeo and Juliet. Jack and Rose on the Titanic. Noah and Allie in The
Notebook. Prince Charming and his princess in every fairy tale ever
told.

But none of those love stories---not a single one---even comes close to
the greatest love story ever told:
The story of Jesus and His church. This is the love story that the entire Bible is building toward. It's
the romance that gives meaning to all other romances. It's the wedding
that every other wedding is simply a shadow of. And you're part of it. But here's where we get confused:
Most Christians think the relationship between Jesus and the individual
believer is like a marriage.

"Jesus is my personal Savior," we say. "I have a personal relationship with Him."
And that's true---but it's not the whole truth. Jesus isn't married to you individually. He's married to the church
collectively. You're not the bride. The church is the bride. You're part of the bride. But you can't be the bride by yourself. I FOUND MY BRIDE IN CHURCH
Speaking of brides, let me tell you how I met Jane. I found her in church.

Not on a dating app. Not at a bar. Not through mutual friends. In church. And you know what? That wasn't an accident. The church isn't just where I met Jesus. It's where I met the love of
my life. It's where I found community. It's where I discovered my
purpose. It's where everything that matters most to me came together. And here's what I've realized over the years:
The church is where you find what you're looking for---even when you
don't know you're looking for it.

I wasn't going to church to find a wife. I was going to church to
worship God. But in the process of pursuing Jesus and being part of His body, I found
Jane. And thousands of people can tell the same story. They came to church
looking for God---and they found friendship, purpose, healing,
community, and yes, sometimes even love. Because the church is where Jesus builds His family. And just like I found my bride in church, Jesus found His bride there
too.

THE COLLECTIVE BRIDE
Here's the imagery that runs throughout Scripture:
Ephesians 5:25-27 -- *Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ
loved the church and gave himself up for her to make her holy, cleansing
her by the washing with water through the word, and to present her to
himself as a radiant church, without stain or wrinkle or any other
blemish, but holy and blameless.*
Revelation 19:7-8 -- *"Let us rejoice and be glad and give him
glory!

For the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made
herself ready. Fine linen, bright and clean, was given her to wear."*
Revelation 21:2 -- *I saw the Holy City, the new Jerusalem, coming
down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride beautifully dressed for
her husband.*
Do you see the pattern? It's never "brides" (plural). It's always "bride" (singular). Jesus isn't coming back for millions of individual brides.

He's coming
back for one bride---the church. You're not marrying Jesus. You're part of the bride that's marrying
Jesus. And that changes everything. WHAT MAKES A BRIDE BEAUTIFUL
Let me take you back to my wedding day. When I stood at the altar and watched Jane walk down the aisle, she was
absolutely stunning. Everything about her was beautiful. The dress. The smile. The way she
looked at me. But you know what made her most beautiful to me?

She was mine. And I was hers. Not because she was perfect. Not because she had no flaws. But because we had committed our lives to each other. Because we had
said yes to each other. Because we were about to become one. That's what makes the church beautiful to Jesus. Not because we're perfect. Not because we have it all together. But because we're His. And He's ours. And one day soon, He's coming back to get us.

JESUS IS EXCITED ABOUT HIS WEDDING DAY
Here's something most Christians don't think about:
Jesus is excited about His wedding day. We talk about the second coming in terms of judgment and tribulation and
end times chaos. But you know what Jesus sees when He thinks about His return? His wedding day. The day He finally gets to take His bride home. The day when everything is made right. The day when the wait is over and the marriage supper of the Lamb
begins.

Jesus can't wait to marry His church. He's not reluctant. He's not disappointed. He's not having second
thoughts. He's counting down the days. And if you're part of His church, you're part of the bride He's
coming back for. THE PROBLEM WITH CRITICIZING THE BRIDE
Now let me bring this home. Remember what I said in Chapter 1 about Jane and the varicose veins? When Jane kept pointing out her flaws, asking me to focus on what she
thought was wrong with her, it bothered me.

Because I didn't see her
that way. I saw her as beautiful. I saw her as mine. The same thing happens when you criticize the church. When you stand outside the church pointing at her flaws---"Look at the
hypocrites! Look at the failures! Look at the mess!"---you're asking
Jesus to focus on the blemishes. You're asking Him to see what's wrong instead of what's beautiful. You're asking Him to separate His love into parts---to love some things
about the church but reject others.

But Jesus doesn't work that way. He sees His bride. And yes, she's imperfect. Yes, she has flaws. Yes,
there are problems. But He loves her anyway. Not because she's perfect, but because she's His. And He's in the process of making her perfect. Ephesians 5:26-27 -- *\...to make her holy, cleansing her by the
washing with water through the word, and to present her to himself as a
radiant church, without stain or wrinkle or any other blemish, but holy
and blameless.*
One day, the church will be perfect.

Radiant. Without spot or wrinkle. But we're not there yet. And that's okay. Because Jesus isn't waiting for the church to be perfect before He
loves her. He loves her now. In the process. In the mess. THE INVITATION
So here's what I want you to understand:
You're invited to the wedding. Not as a spectator. Not as a guest watching from the sidelines. You're invited to be part of the bride. To be part of what Jesus is building.

To be part of the family He's creating. To be part of the body He's preparing to present to Himself. But you can't be the bride by yourself. You need the rest of us. And we need you. That's the whole point. Jesus isn't coming back for isolated individuals who loved Him but
hated His church. He's coming back for a unified bride---the church---that learned to
love each other the way He loved us. THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge for you:
Stop criticizing the bride Jesus is coming back for.

Stop standing on the outside pointing out flaws. Stop saying, "I love Jesus, but not His bride."
And start being part of the bride. If someone told me they loved me but hated Jane, I wouldn't want
anything to do with them. And Jesus feels the same way about His
church. So stop pointing out the blemishes. Stop focusing on what's wrong. And start falling in love with her. Because Jesus already has. And one day soon, He's coming back to get her.

The question is: Will you be part of the bride He's coming back for? Or will you be standing on the outside, criticizing what He gave His
life to create? In the next chapter, we're going to talk about what happens when the
head leaves the body. And spoiler alert: it's not pretty. When you try
to follow Jesus without being connected to His church, you lose
something vital. Let me show you what that is and why it matters more
than you think.*

chapters.title: Chapter 4: When The Head Leaves The Body
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about the most desperate search in human history. When someone goes missing, the first thing authorities do is search for
the body. Not because they're being morbid. Not because they're giving up hope. But because they understand something fundamental: A person and their
body are inseparable. You can't find the person without finding the body. Think about it:
When a loved one dies, families have an overwhelming need to see the
body.

It's why there are viewings. It's why people gather at
gravesides. It's why we cry the most when we see the casket lowered
into the ground. Because you can't separate a person from their body. The body represents the person. The body is where the person lived,
moved, and expressed themselves. Without the body, there's no tangible connection. No physical presence. No way to see or touch or be near the one you love.

Now let me show you why this matters for the church. JESUS HAS A BODY
Here's something most Christians don't think about:
Jesus still has a body. Not the physical body He had 2,000 years ago. That body ascended to
heaven. But He has a body on earth right now. And that body is called the church. Colossians 1:18 -- And he is the head of the body, the church.
1 Corinthians 12:27 -- *Now you are the body of Christ, and each one
of you is a part of it.*
Ephesians 1:22-23 -- *And God placed all things under his feet and
appointed him to be head over everything for the church, which is his
body, the fullness of him who fills everything in every way.*
Read that last verse again.

The church is "the fullness of him who fills everything in every
way."
Not a metaphor. Not a nice illustration. The church is literally the body of Christ on earth. THE HEAD AND THE BODY CANNOT BE SEPARATED
Think about the relationship between a head and a body. Can you separate them? Sure. You can cut off the head. But you know what happens? Both die. The head can't survive without the body. The body can't survive
without the head.

They're inseparable. And that's exactly the relationship between Jesus and His church. You can't have Jesus without His body. You can't have the church
without Jesus as the head. When you try to separate them, both die. Not literally. But spiritually. When you say, "I love Jesus but not the church," you're trying to
separate the head from the body. And when you do that, your faith begins to die. Not immediately.

Not all at once. But gradually. Because you're disconnected from the very thing that gives life. WHAT THE BODY DOES
Let me explain what a body does for a head. The body expresses what the head commands. When your head wants to walk, your legs move. When your head wants to
speak, your mouth opens. When your head wants to embrace someone, your
arms reach out. The body is how the head interacts with the world.

Now apply that to Jesus and the church. Jesus is the head. The church is the body. The church is how Jesus interacts with the world today. When Jesus wants to feed the hungry, He uses the hands of the church. When Jesus wants to speak truth, He uses the mouth of the church. When
Jesus wants to embrace the broken, He uses the arms of the church. This is why the church matters so much. Without the church, Jesus has no physical presence on earth.

He has no hands to serve. No feet to go. No voice to proclaim. The world knows what Jesus thinks by what the church does. Think about that. When people encounter the church, they're encountering the body of
Christ. When they experience love through the church, they're experiencing
Jesus. When they receive grace through the church, they're receiving it
from Jesus. To find the church is to find Jesus. THE EMBODIMENT OF CHRIST
There's a word for this: Embodiment.

To embody something means to give it a body. To make it tangible. To
make it visible. The church is the embodiment of Christ. Jesus is no longer here physically. But He gave us His body---the
church---so that He could still be present, still be active, still be
encountered. John 14:12 -- *"Very truly I tell you, whoever believes in me will
do the works I have been doing, and they will do even greater things
than these, because I am going to the Father."*
How can we do greater works than Jesus?

Because Jesus was limited to one physical body in one location at one
time. But now, through the church, Jesus has millions of bodies in millions of
locations all over the world. The church is the multiplication of Jesus' presence on earth. And you're part of that. THE SEVERED HAND
Let me give you another picture. Imagine you cut off your hand. What happens to the hand? It dies. Not because there's anything wrong with the hand.

But because it's
disconnected from the body. It has no blood flow. No nervous system connection. No life source. A severed hand is a dead hand. Now imagine you're a Christian who's disconnected from the church. You can survive for a while. You might even look alive on the outside. But spiritually, you're like that severed hand. No connection to the body. No flow of life. No nervous system keeping
you in sync with the head.

And eventually, you die. Not physically. But spiritually. Your passion fades. Your faith becomes dry. Your connection to Jesus
weakens. Not because Jesus left you. But because you disconnected from His
body. WHERE TO FIND JESUS TODAY
So if you want to find Jesus today, where do you go? You can encounter Him in your quiet time. In worship. In prayer. In
Scripture. But if you want to find His body, you have to go to the church.

Because that's where His body is. Matthew 18:20 -- *"For where two or three gather in my name, there
am I with them."*
Jesus said, "I am with them."
Not "I might be with them." Not "I'll think about being with them."
"I am with them."
When the church gathers, Jesus is there. In His body. Through His
people. And if you're not connected to the body, you're missing Him. THE MISSING PERSON PRINCIPLE
Remember what I said at the beginning?

When someone goes missing, people don't think of it as a search for a
body, they are looking for a person. Why? Because the body represents the person. When you find the body of Jesus, you find the person of Jesus. And His body is the church. Not a building. Not an institution. Not a denomination. The gathered people of God---that's His body. And when you disconnect from the body, you're disconnecting from Him.

THE INVITATION
So here's the question:
Are you connected to the body? Not just attending occasionally. Not just watching online. Connected. Engaged. Committed. Part of something bigger than yourself. Because here's what happens when you're truly connected:
•  You stop feeling isolated
•  You start experiencing the fullness of Christ
•  You become part of how Jesus interacts with the world
•  You find your purpose and your people
•  You're no longer a severed hand---you're part of a living body
The head and the body cannot be separated.

Jesus and His church are one. And if you want the fullness of Christ, you need the fullness of His
body. THE CHALLENGE
So stop trying to have Jesus without His body. Stop saying, "I can follow Jesus on my own."
Stop disconnecting from the very thing that gives you life. And get connected to the body. Because when the head leaves the body, both die. And when you leave the church, your faith begins to die too.

Jesus is the head. The church is the body. And you can't have one without the other. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something that will
absolutely blow your mind: There's a secret God kept hidden for
thousands of years. Abraham didn't know it. Moses didn't know it. David didn't know it. But now it's been revealed---and you're part of
it. Let me show you the mystery that changes everything.*

chapters.title: Chapter 5: The Secret God Kept For Centuries
paragraphs:
Picture this:
The throne room of hell. A war council. Satan and his principalities and
powers gathered around, strategizing, analyzing, trying to figure out
God's next move. For thousands of years, they thought they had it figured out. They knew the playbook. They understood the game. God had a plan, and
that plan was simple: Israel. God's firstborn son. His chosen people. His beloved nation. Everything in the spiritual realm revolved around that reality.

The
demonic forces knew where to focus their attacks. They knew where to sow
division. They knew where to tempt, to corrupt, to destroy. They thought they had it all mapped out. But then something happened that shocked every demon in hell and stunned
every angel in heaven. God revealed a secret. A mystery. A plan so brilliant, so unexpected, so
absolutely mind-blowing that no one---not even the spiritual powers that
had been watching for millennia---saw it coming.

And that secret was the church. THE PLAYBOOK THEY THOUGHT THEY KNEW
Let me take you back. For thousands of years, the story was clear:
God loved Israel. He chose Abraham. He delivered the Hebrews from Egypt. He made a covenant with Moses. He established David's throne. He spoke
through the prophets. Everything was about Israel. The nations? They were outsiders. Gentiles. The uncircumcised. The ones
without the covenant, without the law, without the promise.

And the spiritual forces---both angelic and demonic---understood this
perfectly. This was the game board. These were the rules. This was how things
worked. Satan focused his attack on God's chosen people. Corrupt them. Lead
them into idolatry. Destroy the line of David. Stop the Messiah from
coming. The angels protected Israel. Fought for them. Ministered to them. Everyone knew their role. Or so they thought. And then Jesus came.

THE COSMIC SHOCK
When Jesus died on the cross, I'm convinced that hell erupted in
celebration. They thought they'd won. They thought they'd killed the Messiah. They thought the plan was over. They thought Israel's hope was dead. But three days later, everything changed. Jesus rose from the dead. The tomb was empty. Death was defeated. And then Jesus did something that nobody expected:
He revealed the mystery.

Ephesians 3:8-10 -- *Although I am less than the least of all the
Lord's people, this grace was given me: to preach to the Gentiles the
boundless riches of Christ, and to make plain to everyone the
administration of this mystery, which for ages past was kept hidden in
God, who created all things. His intent was that now, through the
church, the manifold wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers
and authorities in the heavenly realms.*
Read that again slowly.

"For ages past was kept hidden in God."
Hidden. Secret. Unknown. Even to the spiritual powers watching from the heavenly realms. "His intent was that now, through the church, the manifold wisdom of
God should be made known to the rulers and authorities."
The church was God's secret weapon. The thing nobody saw coming. The plan that was hidden before the foundation of the world. WHAT THE MYSTERY WAS
So what was the mystery?

Here it is:
God's plan was never just about Israel. It was about creating one new
humanity---Jew and Gentile together---united in Christ through the
church. Ephesians 2:14-16 -- *For he himself is our peace, who has made the
two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of
hostility, by setting aside in his flesh the law with its commands and
regulations. His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity out
of the two, thus making peace, and in one body to reconcile both of them
to God through the cross, by which he put to death their hostility.*
Do you see it?

"His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity."
Not just save Israel. Not just save individuals. Create one new humanity. Jew and Gentile together. Slave and free together. Male and female
together. One body. One family. One bride. That was the plan all along. But God kept it hidden. He didn't reveal it to Abraham. He didn't
reveal it to Moses. He didn't reveal it to David. He waited until Jesus. And when Jesus died and rose again, the mystery was revealed:
God loves the world.

And He created the church as His delivery system
of grace to every nation. WHY IT WAS HIDDEN
Now you might be wondering: Why did God keep it secret? Why didn't He just tell everyone from the beginning? Here's what I believe:
If God had revealed His plan, the enemy would have tried to stop it by
not killing Jesus. Jesus had to die in order for God's plan to
succeed.
1 Corinthians 2:7-8 -- *No, we declare God's wisdom, a mystery that
has been hidden and that God destined for our glory before time began.

None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they
would not have crucified the Lord of glory.*
Read that carefully. "If they had understood it, they would not have crucified the Lord of
glory."
The enemy's greatest mistake was crucifying Jesus. Because in killing Jesus, they actually accomplished God's plan. Through the cross, Jesus:
•  Paid for sin
•  Defeated death
•  Destroyed the barrier between Jew and Gentile
•  Created the church
The enemy thought they were stopping God's plan.

But they were
actually fulfilling it. And they didn't see it coming because God kept it hidden. GOD'S FIRSTBORN AND SECONDBORN
Here's another way to think about it:
Exodus 4:22 -- *Then say to Pharaoh, 'This is what the LORD says:
Israel is my firstborn son.'*
God called Israel His firstborn son. But here's what the principalities and powers didn't understand:
God was preparing to adopt a secondborn son---the church.

Romans 8:23 -- *Not only so, but we ourselves, who have the
firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for our
adoption to sonship, the redemption of our bodies.*
Galatians 3:26 -- *So in Christ Jesus you are all children of God
through faith.*
God didn't abandon Israel. But He expanded His family. He brought in the Gentiles. He created the church. He united Jew and
Gentile into one body.

And Jesus became the uniter. The one who brings God's firstborn son (Israel) and God's secondborn
son (the church) together into one family. THE ANGELIC CLASSROOM
Now here's where it gets even more amazing:
Ephesians 3:10 -- *His intent was that now, through the church, the
manifold wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers and
authorities in the heavenly realms.*
Do you know what that means? The angels are learning from the church.

The principalities and powers---both good and evil---are watching the
church and learning things about God they never knew before. They're seeing His wisdom. His creativity. His love. His plan. The church is God's teaching tool for the heavenly realms. Think about that. When angels look at the church---Jews and Gentiles worshiping together,
serving together, loving each other---they're seeing something that was
hidden from them for thousands of years.

They're watching God's mystery unfold in real time. And it's blowing their minds. THE MUSTERION
The Greek word Paul uses for "mystery" is musterion. It doesn't mean a puzzle you can figure out. It means something that
can only be known by divine revelation. You can't figure out a musterion on your own. No amount of human wisdom, intelligence, or study will get you there. God has to open your eyes. Matthew 16:17 -- *Jesus replied, "Blessed are you, Simon son of
Jonah, for this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my
Father in heaven."*
When Peter declared that Jesus was the Messiah, Jesus said: "Flesh
and blood did not reveal that to you."
It was revelation.

A seeing. An opening of the eyes. And understanding the church requires the same thing. You can't figure it out on your own. You need God to open your eyes. You need Him to show you what the church really is. And when He does---when you truly see it---everything changes. THE REVELATION THAT CHANGES EVERYTHING
Let me tell you what happens when God opens your eyes to the mystery of
the church:
You can't unsee it.

Suddenly, you understand why Jesus died. Suddenly, you understand why
the church matters. Suddenly, you understand your purpose. You stop saying, "I love Jesus but not the church."
Because you realize they're inseparable. You stop criticizing the church from the outside. Because you understand she's part of the mystery---the secret plan of
God that's been hidden since before time began. You stop viewing church as optional.

Because you see that you're part of something cosmic, something
eternal, something that even the angels are watching and learning from. It's a revelation that only God can give. And once you have it, you'll never look at the church the same way
again. THE INVITATION INTO THE MYSTERY
So here's the question:
Do you see the mystery? Do you understand that the church isn't just a human organization, but
a divine mystery that was hidden for centuries and is now being
revealed?

Do you see that you're part of something that Abraham, Moses, and David
longed to understand but couldn't fully see? Do you see that the church is the centerpiece of God's plan to display
His wisdom to the universe? If you don't see it yet, ask God to open your eyes. Pray the prayer Paul prayed in Ephesians 1:18 -- *"I pray that the eyes
of your heart may be enlightened in order that you may know the hope to
which he has called you, the riches of his glorious inheritance in his
holy people."*
Ask God to show you the mystery.

Because once you see it, everything changes. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something that might
make you uncomfortable: your couch. That's right---your couch. Because
there's a good chance that your couch is killing your faith. Let me
explain what I mean and why the one thing that separates status from
power in your spiritual life is something most Christians are missing.*

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Why Your Couch Is Killing Your Faith
paragraphs:
Let me start with a question that's going to hit close to home for a
lot of you:
How many Sundays have you spent on your couch instead of in church? I'm not talking about when you were sick. I'm not talking about
legitimate emergencies or unavoidable circumstances. I'm talking about the Sundays when you just\... didn't feel like
going. The Sundays when you hit snooze one too many times. The Sundays when you decided that watching a sermon online in your
pajamas was just as good as being there in person.

The Sundays when you said, "I can worship God at home."
Here's what I want you to know:
Your couch is killing your faith. And I'm not being dramatic. I'm not exaggerating. I'm telling you the
truth that most pastors are too afraid to say. When you choose the couch over the gathering, you're not just missing a
service. You're missing the very thing that separates status from power
in your spiritual life.

Let me explain. THE WORD YOU NEED TO KNOW
There's a Greek word in the New Testament that we translate as
"church."
The word is ekklesia. And here's what it means: The Gathering. Not the building. Not the organization. Not the Sunday service. The gathering. Ekklesia literally means "the called-out ones who assemble together."
It's a word that was used in ancient Athens to describe the governing
assembly. About 40 times a year, citizens would gather to vote on
legislation, make decisions, and govern the city.

The assembly only had power when it gathered. When citizens stayed home, they had status---they were still citizens. But they had no power. No voice. No influence. Power was in the gathering. And that's exactly how the church works. STATUS VS. POWER
Let me give you an illustration from my own life. I serve on a board. When I was appointed to the board, I was given a
title, authority, and responsibility. I had status.

But you know when that status becomes power? When the board gathers. When I'm sitting at home alone, I'm still a board member. I still have
status. But I have no power. Power comes when we gather. When we meet. When we discuss. When we vote. When we act together. The gathering is where status becomes power. And the same is true for you as a Christian. When you accepted Jesus, you received status:
•  You're a child of God
•  You're forgiven
•  You're saved
•  You're part of God's family
But status without gathering has no power.

You're still a Christian when you're sitting on your couch. You still
have your salvation. You still have your relationship with Jesus. But you're powerless. Because power comes in the gathering. THE BIBLICAL EVIDENCE
Don't just take my word for it. Look at what Scripture says:
Acts 2:1 -- *When the day of Pentecost came, they were all together
in one place.*
ALL together. In ONE place. That's when the Holy Spirit fell.

That's when power came. That's when
the church was born. The Holy Spirit could have been poured out on them individually in their
homes. But God waited until they were gathered together in one place in one
accord. Matthew 18:19-20 -- *"Again, truly I tell you that if two of you on
earth agree about anything they ask for, it will be done for them by my
Father in heaven. For where two or three gather in my name, there am I
with them."*
"Where two or three GATHER."
Not "where one sits alone." Not "where someone watches online."
"Where two or three gather."
There's something that happens when we gather that doesn't happen when
we're alone.

Jesus shows up differently. Power is released. Faith is strengthened. The Kingdom advances. YOU ARE NOT THE CHURCH
Let me correct something that's become popular in Christian culture:
"You are the church!"
I hear this all the time. Well-meaning Christians trying to encourage
people that the church isn't a building, it's people. And they're half right. The church is people. But here's what they get wrong:
You are not the church.

WE are the church. The difference matters. When you say "I am the church," you're implying that you can be the
church by yourself. That you don't need anyone else. That gathering is optional. But that's not how it works. The church is a collective body. A community. A gathering. You're a Christian. You're part of the church. But you're not the
church by yourself. Ekklesia requires gathering. Without the gathering, you're a Christian with status but no power.

WHAT YOU LOSE ON THE COUCH
So what do you lose when you choose the couch over the gathering? Let me list a few things:
1. The presence of Jesus in a unique way
Jesus promised to be present when two or three gather. There's a
manifestation of His presence that happens in the gathering that
doesn't happen when you're alone.
2. Corporate worship
There's something powerful about worshiping with other believers.

Something that amplifies your praise. Something that lifts you higher
than you can go alone.
3. Accountability
When you're isolated, there's no one to challenge you, encourage you,
or call you out when you're drifting.
4. Community
The relationships that sustain you through hard times. The friendships
that sharpen you. The connections that remind you you're not alone.
5. Spiritual gifts in operation
When the body gathers, gifts are activated.

Prophecy flows. Teaching
happens. Encouragement is given. Miracles occur.
6. The power of agreement
Jesus said if two agree, it will be done. Agreement requires gathering.
7. Your purpose
You can't fulfill your purpose in isolation. Your gifts are meant to
serve the body. When you're on the couch, your purpose remains dormant. THE 100 FEET UNDERWATER REALITY
Remember my Facebook post from Chapter 1? "You can be a Christian without church like being 100 feet underwater
without breathing---for about 4 minutes."
It's true.

You can survive without breathing---for a very short time. You can survive without the church---for a season. But eventually, you suffocate. Your faith gasps for air. Your passion fades. Your connection to Jesus
weakens. Not because Jesus left you. But because you left the gathering. And without the gathering, you're trying to live as a Christian on your
own strength. Which might work for a little while. But not for long.

THE INVITATION
So here's my challenge for you:
Get off the couch. Stop choosing comfort over community. Stop choosing convenience over commitment. Stop choosing isolation over the gathering. Because here's the truth:
Status without gathering is powerless. You can have all the right theology. You can read your Bible every day. You can pray faithfully. But if you're not gathering with the body, you're missing the power.

The power is in the ekklesia. The power is in the gathering. And if you want to experience everything God has for you---if you want
to tap into the fullness of what it means to be part of His church---you
have to show up. Not just online. Not just when it's convenient. You have to gather. Because the church isn't a couch religion. It's a gathering. And you're part of it. THE CHOICE
So here's the question:
What are you going to choose?

The couch or the gathering? Comfort or community? Status or power? Because here's what I know:
Your couch is comfortable. Your couch is convenient. Your couch doesn't
require anything from you. But your couch is killing your faith. And if you stay there long enough, you'll wake up one day and realize
that your passion is gone, your purpose is unclear, and your faith feels
empty. Not because Jesus left you.

But because you chose status over power. Get off the couch. Join the gathering. Experience the power. Because the church is ekklesia. And ekklesia requires you to show up. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something that should
give you incredible confidence: The church is the last organization
standing. Every government, every empire, every company will eventually
fall. But the church? She's not going anywhere.

Let me show you why the
church will outlast everything on earth---and what that means for you.*

chapters.title: Chapter 7: The Last Organization Standing
paragraphs:
Let me ask you a question that Jane and I had to answer a few years ago:
If we both died, who would we trust with our boys? We were making our living will---the legal document that spells out what
happens to your kids if you're no longer around. And we had to answer the hardest question parents ever face:
Who do we trust with the most precious thing we have? Not just anyone. Not the person with the most money.

Not the person with
the biggest house. The person we trust most. The person who would love them like we love them. Raise them like we
would raise them. Protect them like we would protect them. It's a massive decision. Because you're entrusting your legacy---your
children---to someone else. Now let me show you why I'm telling you this story. Because God faced the same question. And His answer? The church. THE MOST IMPORTANT QUESTION IN HEAVEN
Imagine the conversation in heaven after Jesus ascended:
Jesus has just died, risen, and returned to the Father.

The plan of
salvation is complete. The cross has accomplished its purpose. But now there's a question:
Who's going to guard the truth? Who's going to carry the message? Who's going to preserve the gospel? Who's going to make sure that what Jesus taught doesn't get corrupted,
twisted, or lost? Jesus isn't staying on earth in physical form. The disciples will
eventually die. The apostles won't live forever. So who gets the most precious thing God has---His truth?

And here's God's answer: No-one can. So, let's create a new Entity to
entrust the future with. Jesus when you go to the cross, you won't just
be saving mankind from their sins, you will be creating the organisation
that we will entrust the good news to. And we will call it The church. The church. THE PILLAR AND FOUNDATION OF TRUTH
1 Timothy 3:14-15 -- *Although I hope to come to you soon, I am
writing you these instructions so that, if I am delayed, you will know
how people ought to conduct themselves in God's household, which is the
church of the living God, the pillar and foundation of the truth.*
Read that again.

"The church of the living God, the pillar and foundation of the
truth."
Not "a" pillar. Not "one of many" foundations. THE pillar and THE foundation. Do you know what pillars do? They hold up the house. If the pillars crack, the house collapses. Do you know what foundations do? They support everything built on top. If the foundation crumbles, the
building falls. God is saying: The church is what holds up truth in the world.

Not the government. Not universities. Not media. The church. THE GREEK WORD FOR TRUTH
The word Paul uses for "truth" here is al?theia. It's the same word Jesus used in John 14:6: "I am the way, the
truth, and the life."
Jesus IS the truth. And He entrusted that truth to the church. Not to individuals operating alone. Not to isolated believers doing
their own thing. To the church---the collective body of believers gathered together.

Think about what that means. God looked at all the options:
•  Angels? No.
•  Governments? No.
•  Institutions? No.
•  Individuals? No. The church? Yes. You're part of the organization that God trusts with His most precious
treasure: truth. WHY THE CHURCH? Now you might be wondering: Why the church? Why not something more reliable? Something that doesn't have all the
problems the church has? Here's why:
Because the church has the Holy Spirit.

John 16:13 -- *"But when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will
guide you into all the truth."*
The Holy Spirit lives in the church. He guards the truth. He illuminates Scripture. He convicts when error
creeps in. He preserves what Jesus taught. Without the Holy Spirit, the church would fail. But with the Holy Spirit, the church is indestructible. THE GATES OF HELL
Matthew 16:18 -- *"And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this
rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome
it."*
Let me break this down:
"I WILL build my church."
Not "I might." Not "I hope to."
"I WILL."
It's a promise.

A certainty. A declaration. "And the gates of Hades will not overcome it."
Do you know what gates do? They keep things in or keep things out. Hell has gates. Hell has defenses. Hell has strategies to stop the
advance of God's Kingdom. But those gates will not prevail against the church. Not because the church is perfect. Not because Christians are powerful
on their own. But because Jesus is building it. And what Jesus builds cannot be destroyed.

THE LAST ORGANIZATION STANDING
Let me show you something about history. Every empire falls. Every government eventually collapses. Every company
eventually closes. The Roman Empire? Gone. The British Empire? Dissolved. The Soviet Union? Collapsed. Every political party, every dynasty, every corporation---they all have
an expiration date. Except one organization: the church. The church has outlasted:
•  The Roman emperors who tried to destroy it
•  The Islamic conquests that tried to wipe it out
•  The communist regimes that tried to stamp it out
•  The secular movements that tried to discredit it
Two thousand years later, the church is still here.

Still growing. Still spreading. Still advancing. Because Jesus promised: "I will build my church, and the gates of
hell will not overcome it."
THE HOUSE THAT WILL NEVER FALL
Jesus told a parable about two builders:
One built his house on sand. When the storms came, it collapsed. One built his house on rock. When the storms came, it stood firm. The church is the house built on rock. Not because we're perfect.

But because our foundation is Jesus. And as long as Jesus is the foundation, the church will stand. Governments will rise and fall. Movements will come and go. Trends will
start and end. But the church? She's not going anywhere. Because she's built on the rock that cannot be shaken. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOU
So why does this matter for you? Because you're not just part of any organization. You're part of the organization that will outlast everything on
earth.

You're part of the pillar and foundation of truth. You're part of what Jesus is building. You're part of the only institution that has God's promise: "The
gates of hell will not overcome it."
Everything else will fall. But the church will stand. And when you're part of something that eternal, that indestructible,
that divinely protected---it changes how you see yourself. You're not just a consumer. You're a builder.

You're not just attending. You're investing in something that will
last forever. You're not just showing up. You're part of the advance of God's
Kingdom against the gates of hell. THE TRUST QUESTION REVISITED
Remember the question Jane and I had to answer? Who do we trust with our boys? We chose someone carefully. Someone we knew would guard what's most
precious to us. God faced the same question. Who do I trust with the truth?

And He chose the church. Not because we're perfect. But because He's given us His Holy Spirit. Not because we never fail. But because He's promised to build us. Not because we're strong on our own. But because He's made us the
pillar and foundation. God trusts the church with His most precious treasure. And if God trusts the church, you can too. THE INVITATION
So here's what I want you to see:
You're part of the last organization standing.

When every government has fallen, the church will still be here. When every empire has crumbled, the church will still be advancing. When every company has closed, the church will still be building. Because Jesus is building it. And what Jesus builds cannot be destroyed. So stop treating the church like it's optional. Stop acting like it's just another organization you can take or leave. Stop disconnecting from the very thing that God has entrusted with
truth.

Get connected. Stay committed. Be part of what Jesus is building. Because the church is the pillar and foundation of truth. The church is the house built on rock. The church is the organization that will outlast everything. And you're part of it. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something that will
shock you: What is Jesus' favorite thing? What does He care about most? What was He given all authority FOR?

The answer might surprise you---and
it definitely changes everything.*

chapters.title: Chapter 8: Jesus' Favorite Thing
paragraphs:
Let me ask you a question:
What is Jesus' favorite thing? Not a trick question. I'm genuinely asking. If you could identify the one thing Jesus cares about most, what would
it be? Most Christians would say:
•  "People"
•  "Souls"
•  "Salvation"
•  "The lost"
And those are good answers. Jesus absolutely cares about people. But let me show you something in Scripture that's going to blow your
mind. There's one thing Jesus was given all authority FOR.

Not just authority over. Not just authority about. Authority FOR. And that thing is the church. THE VERSE NOBODY TALKS ABOUT
Ephesians 1:22-23 -- *And God placed all things under his feet and
appointed him to be head over everything FOR the church, which is his
body, the fullness of him who fills everything in every way.*
Read that again slowly. "God appointed him to be head over everything FOR the church."
Not just over the church.

FOR the church. Jesus was given authority over:
•  Governments
•  Kingdoms
•  Powers
•  Spiritual forces
•  All of creation
Why? For the church. Everything Jesus has authority over---every bit of power He
possesses---exists for the benefit of the church. FOR, NOT JUST OVER
Let me explain the difference. If I'm the head over my household, that means I have authority. I'm in charge. I make decisions. But if I'm the head for my household, that means everything I do
with my authority is for their benefit.

My authority isn't just about me having power. It's about me using
that power to serve, protect, provide for, and bless my family. That's what Jesus does for the church. He's not just head over the church. He's head for the church. Every bit of authority He has---every ounce of power---is directed
toward building, protecting, blessing, and advancing the church. WHAT THIS MEANS
So what does it mean that Jesus has all authority for the church?

It means:
1. Jesus uses His authority to build the church
Matthew 16:18 -- "I will build my church."
Not "I might build." Not "I'll try to build."
"I WILL build."
And He uses all His authority to make it happen.
2. Jesus uses His authority to protect the church
He guards the church from the enemy. He defends the truth. He preserves
what He's building.
3. Jesus uses His authority to provide for the church
Every spiritual gift.

Every blessing. Every resource the church
needs---Jesus provides it.
4. Jesus uses His authority to empower the church
He gives the Holy Spirit. He grants authority. He equips believers to do
the work of ministry.
5. Jesus uses His authority to advance the church
He opens doors. He moves in hearts. He breaks down barriers. He causes
the gospel to spread. Everything Jesus has is directed toward the church. WHY THE CHURCH?

Now you might be wondering: Why? Why does Jesus care so much about the church? Why not just save individuals and leave it at that? Here's why:
Because the church is His bride. You don't just save your bride. You cherish her. You protect her. You
provide for her. You do everything in your power to see her flourish. Ephesians 5:25 -- *Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved
the church and gave himself up for her.*
Jesus didn't die just to save you.

He died to create a bride. And now He's using all His authority to prepare her, protect her, and
present her as radiant and holy. That's His favorite thing. THE PRIORITY OF HEAVEN
Here's what I need you to understand:
The church is the priority of heaven. Not an afterthought. Not a side project. Not Plan B. The church is what Jesus is focused on. Every move of the Holy Spirit? For the church. Every spiritual gift distributed?

For the church. Every door opened? For the church. Every prayer answered? For the church. Jesus has made the church His priority. And if the church is Jesus' priority, it should be yours too. THE FULLNESS OF CHRIST
Look at the end of that verse again:
Ephesians 1:23 -- *"\...the church, which is his body, the fullness
of him who fills everything in every way."*
"The fullness of him who fills everything in every way."
The church isn't just part of what Jesus is doing.

The church is the fullness of what Jesus is doing. You can't separate Jesus from His body. You can't understand Jesus' mission without understanding the church. You can't experience the fullness of Christ without being connected to
His body. The church is where the fullness of Jesus is found. WHAT YOU'RE PART OF
So here's what this means for you:
When you're part of the church, you're part of Jesus' favorite thing.

You're part of what He gave His life to create. You're part of what He uses all His authority for. You're part of the fullness of Him who fills everything. This isn't small. This isn't insignificant. You're part of the most important thing happening on planet earth. THE DISCONNECT
So why do so many Christians treat the church like it's optional? Why do we say, "I love Jesus, but I don't need the church"? Because we don't understand what Jesus cares about most.

We think Jesus is all about individuals. Personal relationships. Private
faith. But Scripture shows us something different:
Jesus is all about the church. Yes, He loves you individually. Yes, He died for your sins personally. But His mission is corporate, not just individual. He's building a body. A family. A bride. And you're part of it. ALIGNING WITH JESUS' PRIORITY
So here's the question:
Are you aligned with Jesus' priority?

If Jesus' favorite thing is the church, what's yours? If Jesus uses all His authority for the church, how are you using yours? If Jesus is building the church, are you helping or hindering? You can't claim to follow Jesus while ignoring His favorite thing. You can't say you love Him while rejecting what He loves most. You can't be aligned with His mission while disconnecting from His
body. If Jesus' priority is the church, your priority should be the church
too.

THE INVITATION
So here's what I'm asking you to do:
Make the church your priority. Not just something you attend when it's convenient. Not just a box you check. Not just a place you go on Sundays. Your priority. Because the church is Jesus' favorite thing. And if you love Jesus, you'll love what He loves. If you follow Jesus, you'll prioritize what He prioritizes. If you're aligned with Jesus, you'll invest in what He's invested in.

Jesus has all authority---and He uses it FOR the church. Are you using what you have for the church too? In the next chapter, we're going to talk about how grace gets from
heaven to your street address. Because there's a delivery system---and
you're part of it. Let me show you why the church is God's delivery
service and what happens when you disconnect from it.*

chapters.title: Chapter 9: God's Delivery Service
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about the most frustrating feeling in the world:
Having what someone needs---but no way to get it to them. Imagine you're a doctor in the ER. A patient is dying in front of you. You know exactly what they need---the medicine is right there in a vial
on the counter. But there's no syringe. No IV. No way to deliver it. The cure exists. But without a delivery system, the patient dies. Or imagine you're on an airplane that suddenly loses cabin pressure.

The oxygen is there---gallons of it pumping through the plane. But when
you reach for the mask at your seat, it's not there. The oxygen exists,
but without the mask to deliver it, you suffocate. Or think about Amazon. You can browse millions of products online. Beautiful website. Easy checkout. But if those blue delivery trucks
didn't exist? Nothing would ever reach your door. The products exist,
but without the trucks, you'd never get what you ordered.

That's the picture I want you to see:
Grace exists. God's love, forgiveness, healing, hope, peace,
joy---it's all available. It's real. It's powerful. But without a delivery system, it never reaches the people who need it
most. And here's what most Christians don't understand:
The church is God's delivery system. Let me show you what I mean. THE PROBLEM BEFORE THE CHURCH
Here's something that might surprise you:
For most of human history, there was no church.

That's not a typo. The church didn't exist in the Old Testament. God dealt directly with individuals:
•  Adam and Eve in the garden
•  Noah and the ark
•  Abraham and the covenant
•  Moses on the mountain
•  David in the palace
And God dealt with one nation: Israel. But the Gentiles? The rest of the world? They were on the outside. Ephesians 2:11-12 -- *Remember that at that time you were separate
from Christ, excluded from citizenship in Israel and foreigners to the
covenants of the promise, without hope and without God in the world.*
Without hope.

Without God. Without a delivery system. The grace of God existed. But for thousands of years, most of the world
had no way to access it. THEN JESUS CHANGED EVERYTHING
Ephesians 3:6 -- *This mystery is that through the gospel the
Gentiles are heirs together with Israel, members together of one body,
and sharers together in the promise in Christ Jesus.*
Read that again slowly. Heirs together. Members together.

Sharers together. Something radical happened when Jesus died on the cross and rose from
the dead:
God created a delivery system for grace. That delivery system is called the church. Not a building. Not a denomination. Not an organization. The church---the gathered people of God---became the vehicle through
which grace flows from heaven to earth. Think about it:
How did you hear about Jesus? Someone told you.

How did you learn to
pray? Someone taught you. How did you experience God's love? Someone showed you. That's the church functioning as God's delivery system. THE BANK MANAGER STORY
Let me tell you about a woman I met when I first came to the United
States. She was my first bank manager---so nice, so helpful. Over the next 10
years, I kept in touch with her. Every now and then, she'd reach out to
talk about some personal challenges she was facing.

I invited her to church multiple times. But she always found a way not
to attend. "Maybe next week." "I'm too busy." "I'll think about it."
And honestly? I didn't push too hard. I just stayed in her life. Prayed
for her. Kept the door open. Then, just before we opened our new campus in Alpharetta, she called me. It was Thanksgiving. Her father had just died suddenly. And in that
moment of grief and confusion, she didn't know who to call.

So she called me. We talked. I listened. I prayed with her. I told her I was there for
her. A couple of weeks later, I felt prompted to invite her to church again. Opening Sunday of the new Alpharetta campus. And this time, she said
yes. She came. And that day, with tears flowing down her face, she surrendered her life
to Jesus. Everything changed. Suddenly, joy replaced her grief. Peace replaced her anxiety. Community
replaced her isolation.

Her life was completely transformed. And now, a few years later? She's an amazing carrier of grace herself. She attends church regularly. She serves with great joy and enthusiasm. Recently, she told me something powerful:
"My girlfriends keep asking me what's changed. They can tell I'm a
different person. And I get to tell them it's Jesus---and His
church."
That's the power of God's delivery system. THE INN METAPHOR
You know the story of the Good Samaritan, right?

A man was beaten, robbed, and left for dead on the side of the road. Religious leaders passed by without helping. But then a Samaritan---an
outsider---stopped. Here's what the Samaritan did:
He bandaged the man's wounds (first aid). He put him on his
donkey (like loading someone in your car today). He took him to an
inn (he didn't just help him on the road and leave). He paid for
the man's care and recovery.

Now here's the key: The man wasn't healed on the road. Yes, the Samaritan stopped the bleeding. Yes, he provided immediate
care. But healing? Recovery? Transformation? That happened at the inn. Do you see what Jesus is showing us? The church is the inn. You can encounter Jesus on the road. You can have a moment where someone
prays for you, encourages you, points you to Christ. But healing happens at the inn. Transformation happens in community.

Recovery happens when you're connected to the body. The church is where wounded people come to be healed. And here's the beautiful part:
My bank manager? I found her on the road. I bandaged her wounds. I
prayed with her over the phone. But she wasn't healed on the road. She was healed at the inn. She was healed when she came to church, encountered the presence of God,
experienced the love of His people, and plugged into community.

And now? She's not just someone who was healed at the inn. She's one of the innkeepers. She's part of God's delivery system now helping bring grace to other
people who are wounded on the side of the road. YOU'RE BOTH RECEIVER AND DELIVERER
Here's what I need you to understand:
You didn't just receive grace so you could keep it. You received grace so you could deliver it. You're not just a customer at the inn.

You're not just a patient
receiving care. You're part of the delivery team. When you connect to the church, you're plugging into God's delivery
system. Grace flows to you. But it's also supposed to flow through
you. Think about it:
•  Who needs the hope you've received?
•  Who needs the peace you've experienced?
•  Who needs the love you've been shown?
•  Who's lying wounded on the side of the road, waiting for someone to
bring them to the inn?

That's your job. Not the pastor's job. Not the staff's job. Your job. Because the church isn't just a place where you receive. It's a place
where you become a deliverer. WHAT YOU LOSE WHEN YOU DISCONNECT
So here's what happens when you disconnect from the church:
You cut yourself off from the delivery system. Not immediately. Not all at once. But gradually. The grace dries up. The community disappears. The accountability fades.

The mission becomes unclear. And eventually, you find yourself isolated, disconnected, and wondering
why your faith feels empty. But here's what's even worse:
When you disconnect, you stop being part of the delivery system for
others. Someone out there is dying without the cure---they need the injection
you could provide. Someone is suffocating without oxygen---they need the mask you could
offer. Someone is waiting for a package---they need the blue truck you could
drive.

And when you're not connected to the church, you can't be part of the
delivery. THE THREE DELIVERY SYSTEMS REVISITED
Let me bring those opening illustrations back:
The ER injection:
•  The medicine exists (God's grace)
•  The patient is dying (people without Christ)
•  The injection is the delivery system (the church)
•  Without the injection, the medicine never reaches the patient
The airplane oxygen mask:
•  The oxygen exists (the Holy Spirit)
•  The passengers are suffocating (people gasping for life)
•  The mask is the delivery system (the church)
•  Without the mask, the oxygen never reaches the person
The Amazon blue truck:
•  The products exist (God's blessings)
•  The customer is waiting (people in need)
•  The truck is the delivery system (the church)
•  Without the truck, the package never arrives
In every case, the church is essential.

Not because grace doesn't exist without the church. But because grace doesn't reach people without the church. THE CHALLENGE
So let me ask you:
Are you connected to God's delivery system? Not just attending. Not just showing up occasionally. Connected. Plugged in. Engaged. Serving. Giving. Participating in the mission. Because someone is dying right now, and the cure exists. But they need
someone to deliver it.

Someone is suffocating right now, and the oxygen is available. But they
need a mask. Someone is desperate right now, and the product they need is ready. But
they need a delivery truck. The church is God's delivery system. And you're part of it. So stop sitting on the sidelines. Stop watching from a distance. Stop disconnecting from the very thing that brings grace to the world. And start delivering. Because someone is lying wounded on the side of the road, waiting for
someone to bring them to the inn.

And you might be the one God wants to use. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something that should
both terrify us and give us incredible urgency: What happens if the
church fails? What if we don't show up? What if we don't fulfill our
mission? Let me tell you why God doesn't have a Plan B---and what that
means for you and me.*

chapters.title: Chapter 10: Plan A (There Is No Plan B)
paragraphs:
