Let me tell you something that should terrify us:
If the church fails, there is no backup plan. God isn't sitting in heaven with a Plan B in His back pocket, just in
case the church doesn't work out. There's no angelic rescue mission waiting in the wings. There's no divine alternative if we drop the ball. The church is Plan A. And there is no Plan B. Let me show you why this matters more than you think. THE GREAT COMMISSION
Matthew 28:18-20 -- *Then Jesus came to them and said, "All
authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.

Therefore go and
make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father
and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, and teaching them to obey
everything I have commanded you. And surely I am with you always, to the
very end of the age."*
This is the Great Commission. The marching orders for the church. And notice what Jesus didn't say:
He didn't say, "Try to make disciples, and if it doesn't work out,
I'll send angels to finish the job."
He didn't say, "Do your best, and I'll have a backup plan ready."
He said, "Go and make disciples of all nations."
Period.

End of sentence. That's the plan. And it's the only plan. THE WEIGHT OF RESPONSIBILITY
Do you understand what that means? The salvation of the world depends on the church. Not on angels preaching the gospel. Not on supernatural signs in the
sky. Not on God writing messages in the clouds. On the church. On you and me showing up. On us being the hands and feet of Jesus. On us
delivering the message. If we don't do it, it doesn't get done.

Now, before you panic, let me clarify:
God is still sovereign. Jesus is still building His church. The Holy
Spirit is still at work. But God has chosen to work through the church. And if the church doesn't fulfill its mission, people die without
hearing the gospel. WHY NO PLAN B? So why doesn't God have a Plan B? Why put all His eggs in one basket? Here's why:
Because Plan A works. Acts 2:41 -- *Those who accepted his message were baptized, and
about three thousand were added to their number that day.*
Acts 4:4 -- *But many who heard the message believed; so the number
of men who believed grew to about five thousand.*
Acts 6:7 -- *So the word of God spread.

The number of disciples in
Jerusalem increased rapidly, and a large number of priests became
obedient to the faith.*
The church worked in the first century. And it's been working for 2,000 years. Millions of people have come to Christ through the church. Countless lives have been transformed through the body of Christ. The gospel has spread to every continent through the faithful witness of
believers. Plan A is working.

God doesn't need a Plan B. He just needs us to stay faithful to Plan A. WHAT HAPPENS IF WE FAIL? But here's the sobering reality:
If the church doesn't fulfill its mission, people don't hear the
gospel. Romans 10:13-14 -- *"Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord
will be saved." How, then, can they call on the one they have not
believed in? And how can they believe in the one of whom they have not
heard?

And how can they hear without someone preaching to them?*
Read that carefully. "How can they hear without someone preaching to them?"
The answer? They can't. If we don't preach, they don't hear. If they don't hear, they can't
believe. If they can't believe, they can't be saved. The chain breaks if we fail. And there's no Plan B to pick up the slack. THE URGENCY OF THE MISSION
So here's what I need you to understand:
This isn't optional.

This isn't a hobby. This isn't something you
do when you have extra time. This is the mission. And people's eternal destinies depend on it. Right now, someone is dying without Christ. Right now, someone is searching for hope. Right now, someone is one conversation away from surrendering to Jesus. And you might be the one God wants to use. Not because you're special. But because you're available. Not because you're perfect.

But because you're willing. You're Plan A. And there is no Plan B. SPIRITUAL GIFTS: THE BODY GETTING IN SHAPE
Now let me show you how Plan A actually works:
Through spiritual gifts.
1 Corinthians 12:12, 27 -- *Just as a body, though one, has many
parts, but all its many parts form one body, so it is with Christ\... Now you are the body of Christ, and each one of you is a part of it.*
The church is a body.

And every part has a function. You're not a spectator. You're not in the audience. You're a body part. And when you don't use your gift, the body is weaker. Romans 12:6-8 -- *We have different gifts, according to the grace
given to each of us. If your gift is prophesying, then prophesy in
accordance with your faith; if it is serving, then serve; if it is
teaching, then teach; if it is to encourage, then give encouragement; if
it is giving, then give generously; if it is to lead, do it diligently;
if it is to show mercy, do it cheerfully.*
Every believer has a gift.

A function. A role to play. And when you don't use your gift, you're limiting what God can do
through His church. The body needs to get in shape. Not because God is weak. But because He's chosen to work through His
body. And if the body isn't functioning, the mission stalls. MY MOTORBIKE STORY
Let me give you a personal example. When I was dating Jane, I was young and fit. I used to ride
motorbikes---dirt bikes, motocross bikes.

I could handle anything. I'd fly over jumps. Tear around corners. My body could do whatever my
head wanted it to do. The head commanded, and the body responded. Fast forward to when I was 60 years old. My son wanted to go to a
motocross and ATV track. We went. And I sat there watching. My head was saying, "Come on! You can do those jumps. You used to be
able to!"
But my body was saying, "Don't you dare. I can't handle that
anymore."
My head was willing, but my body wasn't ready.

Now let me connect this to the church:
If the church is the body and Jesus is the head, we think we're
waiting for Jesus to move. But the reality is: He's actually waiting for us. Jesus---the head of the church---is ready. He has all authority. He has
all power. He has the vision. He wants to reach nations. He wants to heal the broken. He wants to set
captives free. But our body is not up to scratch. The body is small.

The body is out of shape. The body is disconnected. The body is weak. God can't do all He wants to do---not because He lacks the power, but
because His body isn't ready. THE HEAD IS READY
Let me say it plainly:
Jesus is ready to move. The head is willing. The head has all authority. The head is prepared. But the body needs to get in shape. And that happens when every part of the body starts functioning. When you use your gifts.

When you serve. When you give. When you engage. That's when the body gets strong enough to do what the head wants to
do. NO EXCUSES
So here's what I'm hearing some of you say:
"But I'm not qualified." "But I don't have anything to offer."
"But I'm not gifted like other people."
Stop. Every believer has a gift.
1 Peter 4:10 -- *Each of you should use whatever gift you have
received to serve others, as faithful stewards of God's grace in its
various forms.*
You have a gift.

You have a function. You have something to contribute. And when you say, "I'm not qualified," what you're really saying is:
"My part of the body doesn't need to function."
And that weakens the entire body. THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge to you:
Stop sitting on the sidelines. Stop thinking someone else will do it. Stop waiting for the "right time" to get involved. You're Plan A. And there is no Plan B.

People are dying without Christ. The world is waiting for the gospel. The mission is urgent. And God is waiting for His body to get in shape so He can do what He
wants to do through us. The head is ready. The body just needs to get in shape. So get connected. Find your gift. Start serving. Start giving. Start using what God has given you. Because you're part of Plan A. And there is no Plan B. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about the day everything
changed.

There's a specific moment in history when God changed the
rules of how He works in the world. And if you don't understand that
moment, you'll miss the entire point of the church. Let me show you
what happened and why it matters.*

chapters.title: Chapter 11: The Day God Changed The Rules
paragraphs:
Imagine you're playing a game. You know the rules. You've mastered the strategy. You've figured out
how to win. And then, right in the middle of the game, someone changes all the
rules. Everything you thought you knew? Doesn't apply anymore. Everything you've been working toward? Doesn't matter anymore. The entire game has changed. That's exactly what happened when Jesus came. God changed the rules. And the church was His trump card---the game-changer that nobody saw
coming.

Let me show you what I mean. THE OLD RULES
For thousands of years, God had one way of dealing with humanity:
The Law. God gave the Law to Moses on Mount Sinai. 613 commandments that covered
everything from worship to diet to relationships to cleanliness. And the deal was simple:
Obey the Law, and you'll be blessed. Disobey the Law, and you'll be
cursed. That was the game. Those were the rules. And the Jewish people spent centuries trying to follow those rules.

They
built their entire identity around the Law. They had priests who offered sacrifices. They had a temple where God's
presence dwelt. They had rituals, festivals, and traditions. Everything was about keeping the Law. And the rest of the world? The Gentiles? They weren't part of the game. They didn't have the Law. They didn't
have access to God. They were on the outside looking in. THEN JESUS CAME
John 1:17 -- *For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth
came through Jesus Christ.*
Do you see what that verse is saying?

The Law came through Moses. Grace came through Jesus. Two different systems. Two different ways of relating to God. Under the Law:
•  You had to earn God's favor by obedience
•  Sacrifices had to be made for sin
•  Only priests could enter God's presence
•  Only Israel had access to God
Under Grace:
•  God's favor is a gift, not something earned
•  Jesus made the final sacrifice
•  Every believer has access to God's presence
•  The whole world is invited
The rules changed.

And the game was never the same. THE TRUMP CARD
Now here's where the church comes in. When God changed the rules from Law to Grace, He needed a new system. Under the Law, you had:
•  The temple (where God's presence dwelt)
•  The priesthood (who mediated between God and people)
•  The sacrifices (that dealt with sin)
But under Grace, all of that changed. And God's new system was the church. Instead of a temple made of stone, God created a temple made of people.
1 Corinthians 3:16 -- *Don't you know that you yourselves are
God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in your midst?*
Instead of a priesthood that mediated between God and people, God made
every believer a priest.
1 Peter 2:9 -- *But you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a
holy nation, God's special possession.*
Instead of repeated sacrifices, Jesus made one sacrifice---once for all.

Hebrews 10:10 -- *And by that will, we have been made holy through
the sacrifice of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.*
The church was God's trump card. The thing nobody expected. The new system that replaced the old. BREAKING DOWN THE WALL
Under the old rules, there was a wall between Jew and Gentile. The Gentiles were excluded. Outsiders. Separated from the covenants and
promises. But when God changed the rules, He tore down that wall.

Ephesians 2:14-16 -- *For he himself is our peace, who has made the
two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of
hostility, by setting aside in his flesh the law with its commands and
regulations. His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity out
of the two, thus making peace, and in one body to reconcile both of them
to God through the cross, by which he put to death their hostility.*
Read that carefully.

"He destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility."
"His purpose was to create in himself one new humanity out of the
two."
"And in one body to reconcile both of them to God."
The wall is gone. The separation is over. Jew and Gentile are now one in Christ. And that unity is found in the church. THE NEW GAME BOARD
So what does the new game look like? Under the old rules:
•  God dealt with one nation (Israel)
•  Access to God was limited to the temple
•  Priests mediated between God and people
•  The Law defined righteousness
Under the new rules:
•  God deals with all nations (through the church)
•  Access to God is available to all believers
•  Jesus is the one mediator between God and people
•  Grace defines righteousness
The church is the centerpiece of the new system.

Not because we're special. But because God chose to work this way. He could have kept the old system. He could have stuck with Israel
alone. But He changed the rules. And the church was born. WHY THE CHANGE? So why did God change the rules? Why replace the Law with Grace? Why create the church? Here's why:
Because the Law couldn't save anyone. Romans 3:20 -- *Therefore no one will be declared righteous in
God's sight by the works of the law; rather, through the law we become
conscious of our sin.*
The Law showed us our sin.

But it couldn't save us from it. The Law pointed to our need for grace. But it couldn't provide grace. The Law was never meant to save us. It was meant to show us our need
for a Savior. And when Jesus came, the Savior arrived. Grace replaced Law. Freedom replaced bondage. Relationship replaced
ritual. And the church became the vehicle through which grace flows to the
world. THE SHOCK TO THE RELIGIOUS SYSTEM
Now imagine how shocking this was to the religious leaders of Jesus'
day.

They had built their entire lives around the Law. They had mastered the
system. They knew how to play the game. And then Jesus showed up and said, "The rules have changed."
No wonder they were furious. No wonder they opposed Him. No wonder they crucified Him. Because He was dismantling everything they had built their lives on. But here's the irony:
By crucifying Jesus, they actually accomplished God's plan.

Through the cross, Jesus:
•  Fulfilled the Law
•  Paid for sin
•  Destroyed the dividing wall
•  Created the church
The religious leaders thought they were stopping Jesus. But they were
actually helping Him change the rules. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOU
So why does this matter for you? Because you need to understand what system you're living under. Are you trying to earn God's favor by good behavior? That's the old
system.

Are you trying to follow a list of rules to be righteous? That's the
old system. Are you trying to work your way into God's presence? That's the old
system. The rules have changed. You don't earn God's favor. You receive it as a gift. You don't become righteous by following rules. You're made righteous
by faith in Jesus. You don't work your way into God's presence. You're invited in
through grace. And all of this happens in and through the church.

Because the church is the new system God created when He changed the
rules. THE TRUMP CARD IN ACTION
Let me bring this full circle:
The church is God's trump card. When everyone thought God was only interested in Israel, He revealed the
church---a body made up of people from every nation. When everyone thought you had to keep the Law to be righteous, He
revealed grace---a gift given freely through faith.

When everyone thought God's presence was confined to a temple, He
revealed the church---a living temple made of people. The church was the game-changer. And it still is. THE INVITATION
So here's what I'm asking you to do:
Stop trying to play by the old rules. Stop trying to earn what's already been given. Stop trying to achieve what's already been accomplished. The rules have changed. And the new rules say:
•  Grace is a gift
•  Righteousness comes by faith
•  Access to God is open to all
•  The church is God's new system
You're part of the new game.

So play by the new rules. Live in grace. Walk in freedom. Be part of the church. Because God changed the rules. And the church is His trump card. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something powerful:
What you can do together that you could never do alone. There's a power
that's released when believers gather that doesn't exist in isolation. Let me show you what it is and how you can tap into it.*

chapters.title: Chapter 12: The Power Only We Have
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about something you can't do alone:
Experience the fullness of God's power. You can have a personal relationship with Jesus. You can pray. You can
worship. You can read Scripture. But there's a power that only exists in the collective. A power that's released when believers gather that doesn't exist when
you're isolated. A power you can access together that you could never access alone. Let me show you what I mean.

THE COAL IN THE FIRE
There's an old illustration that perfectly captures this truth:
Imagine a coal that's burning in a fire. It's hot. It's glowing. It's full of energy. Now take that coal out of the fire and set it aside by itself. What happens? It goes cold. Not immediately. For a little while, it stays warm. It might even keep
glowing for a few minutes. But eventually, without the other coals around it, it loses its heat.

Isolated, it dies. But put that coal back in the fire---surrounded by other burning
coals---and what happens? It reignites. It glows again. It burns hot. Not because there's anything wrong with the coal. But because fire
needs community to stay burning. That's exactly how it works with believers and the church. You can stay spiritually alive for a season on your own. But eventually, without the community of other believers, your fire
starts to go out.

Your passion fades. Your faith cools. Your connection to God weakens. Not because Jesus left you. But because you isolated yourself from the
fire. THE POWER OF AGREEMENT
Here's one of the most powerful promises Jesus ever made:
Matthew 18:19-20 -- *"Again, truly I tell you that if two of you on
earth agree about anything they ask for, it will be done for them by my
Father in heaven. For where two or three gather in my name, there am I
with them."*
Read that again.

"If TWO of you agree\..."
Not one. Not a single person praying alone. Two. There's a power in agreement that doesn't exist in isolation. When two believers come together and agree in prayer, Jesus says, "It
will be done for them by my Father in heaven."
That's corporate power. Power that only exists when believers gather. THE ACTS 2:42 DEVOTION
Let me show you what the early church looked like:
Acts 2:42 -- *They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and
to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer.*
"They devoted themselves."
Not "They attended occasionally." Not "They showed up when it was
convenient."
"They devoted themselves."
The word "devoted" means persistent, constant, committed.

And what did they devote themselves to?
1. The apostles' teaching -- They learned together
2. Fellowship -- They did life together
3. Breaking of bread -- They ate together, worshiped together
4. Prayer -- They sought God together
This wasn't a solo faith. This was a community faith. And look at what happened:
Acts 2:43-47 -- *Everyone was filled with awe at the many wonders
and signs performed by the apostles.

All the believers were together and
had everything in common. They sold property and possessions to give to
anyone who had need. Every day they continued to meet together in the
temple courts. They broke bread in their homes and ate together with
glad and sincere hearts, praising God and enjoying the favor of all the
people. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being
saved.*
"Every day they continued to meet together."
Not once a week.

Not when they felt like it. Every day. And the result?
•  Wonders and signs
•  Generosity and provision
•  Joy and unity
•  Growth and salvation
That's the power only WE have. WHAT YOU CAN'T DO ALONE
Let me list some things you simply can't do by yourself:
1. You can't worship corporately
There's something about lifting your voice with hundreds or thousands
of other believers that amplifies praise. Something that happens when voices unite that doesn't happen when you
sing alone in your car.
2.

You can't experience spiritual gifts in isolation
Prophecy, teaching, encouragement, healing, administration---these gifts
are designed to operate in the body. When you're alone, your gifts stay dormant.
3. You can't sharpen yourself
Proverbs 27:17 -- *As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens
another.*
You need other believers to challenge you, correct you, and help you
grow.
4. You can't bear each other's burdens
Galatians 6:2 -- *Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you
will fulfill the law of Christ.*
When you're isolated, you carry your burdens alone.

And they crush you.
5. You can't experience unity
Unity requires more than one person. And Jesus prayed for unity among
believers because He knew it would be a powerful testimony to the world.
6. You can't fulfill the Great Commission
Jesus said, "Go and make disciples."
That's a corporate mission. One person can't disciple all nations. But
the church can. All of these require community. All of these require gathering.

All of
these are powers only WE have. THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN ALONE AND TOGETHER
Let me give you a practical example. When I pray alone, God hears me. He answers. He moves. But when the church prays together, something different happens. Acts 4:31 -- *After they prayed, the place where they were meeting
was shaken. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the
word of God boldly.*
"After they prayed, the place was shaken."
Not a quiet, private moment.

A shaking. A manifestation of power. That's what happens when believers pray together. When I worship alone, I encounter God. It's beautiful. It's personal. But when the church worships together, heaven invades earth. There's a dimension of God's presence that's only accessible in
corporate worship. That's the power only WE have. THE ENEMY'S STRATEGY
Now let me show you why the enemy works so hard to keep you isolated:
Because he knows the power of the gathering.

Satan doesn't care if you pray alone. He doesn't care if you read your
Bible at home. He doesn't care if you listen to worship music in your
car. But he's terrified of the gathered church. Because when believers gather:
•  Faith multiplies
•  Power is released
•  The enemy's schemes are exposed
•  Strongholds are broken
•  The Kingdom advances
That's why he works so hard to keep you disconnected. Offense?

That's a tactic to isolate you. Disappointment? That's a
strategy to pull you away. Busyness? That's a distraction to keep you
from gathering. The enemy knows that an isolated believer is a weak believer. But a believer connected to the body? That's a force to be reckoned
with. THE MULTIPLICATION EFFECT
Here's something else about corporate power:
It multiplies. Deuteronomy 32:30 -- *How could one man chase a thousand, or two put
ten thousand to flight, unless their Rock had sold them, unless the LORD
had given them up?*
Do the math:
One person can chase 1,000.

But two people don't chase 2,000---they chase 10,000. That's the multiplication effect of unity. When you come together with other believers, the power doesn't just
add---it multiplies. Your faith multiplies. Your impact multiplies. Your effectiveness
multiplies. That's the power only WE have. THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge to you:
Stop trying to do this alone. Stop thinking you can experience the fullness of God's power in
isolation.

Stop being the coal outside the fire. Get back in the fire. Devote yourself to the church. Not just attend---devote. Not just show up---commit. Not just participate---invest. Because there's a power that only exists in the collective. A power you can access together that you could never access alone. It's the power only WE have. And you're part of it. THE INVITATION
Let me end with this:
You're not meant to do this Christian life alone.

You're not meant to carry your burdens by yourself. You're not meant to fight your battles in isolation. You're meant to be part of a body. A community. A family. A fire of believers burning together. And when you're connected to that body, everything changes:
•  Your faith grows stronger
•  Your burdens become lighter
•  Your impact becomes greater
•  Your power multiplies
That's the power only WE have.

So stop trying to go it alone. Join the fire. Stay in the body. Experience the power. Because there's something you can do together that you could never do
alone. And it's time to experience it. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about something deeply
personal: Why your feelings about the church reveal everything about
your relationship with Jesus. Because if you truly see who Jesus is,
you'll fall in love with what He loves.

Let me show you why falling in
love with the church isn't optional---it's inevitable when you
encounter Jesus.*

chapters.title: Chapter 13: Fall In Love Or Fall Away
paragraphs:
Let me tell you something that's going to sound harsh:
If you don't love the church, you don't yet have a revelation of
Jesus. I can already feel some of you getting defensive. "Wait a minute---I
love Jesus! I'm a Christian! How dare you question my faith?"
But hear me out. Because what I'm about to say isn't judgment. It's observation. When you truly see who Jesus is, you receive His heart. And His heart
beats for the church.

Let me explain. THE REVELATION PRINCIPLE
Matthew 16:15-17 -- *"But what about you?" he asked. "Who do you
say I am?" Simon Peter answered, "You are the Messiah, the Son of the
living God." Jesus replied, "Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah, for
this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my Father in
heaven."*
When Peter declared that Jesus was the Messiah, Jesus didn't say,
"Good guess."
He didn't say, "You figured it out."
He said, "This was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my
Father in heaven."
It was a revelation.

Something Peter couldn't figure out on his own. Something that required
God to open his eyes. And here's what happens when God opens your eyes to see Jesus:
You receive His heart. It's like a download. In one moment, you don't just learn about
Jesus---you receive His passions, His priorities, His loves. And Jesus loves the church. So when God reveals Jesus to you, you don't just see Jesus. You see
what Jesus sees.

You love what Jesus loves. You can't have a revelation of Jesus without receiving His heart for
the church. MY ENCOUNTER WITH JESUS
Let me tell you about the moment everything changed for me. I had a personal encounter with Jesus that I can only describe as
overwhelming. It was like a download of 4 trillion gigabytes of information all at
once. In that moment, I understood things I'd never understood before.

I saw
things I'd never seen before. I received things I'd never received
before. But here's what's important:
In that download---He didn't just download His love for me. He
downloaded His love for the church. I didn't just encounter Jesus and say, "Wow, I love Jesus!"
I encountered Jesus and said, "Wow, I love Jesus---and I love what He
loves."
It wasn't optional. It wasn't separate. It was part of the
download.

When you truly see Jesus, you receive His heart. And His heart beats for
the church. THE LITMUS TEST
So here's the litmus test:
Your feelings about the church reveal your revelation of Jesus. If you say, "I love Jesus but not the church," here's what you're
really saying:
"I haven't yet fully seen who Jesus is."
Not because you're a bad Christian. Not because you're not saved. But because a full revelation of Jesus includes His heart for the
church.

Let me say it another way:
•  If you see Jesus as your personal Savior, you'll be grateful to Him
•  If you see Jesus as your Lord, you'll obey Him
•  If you see Jesus as the head of the church, you'll love what He's
building
The deeper your revelation, the stronger your love for the church. YOU'LL NEVER ABANDON WHAT YOU TRULY SEE
Here's a principle that applies to everything in life:
You'll never abandon what you truly see.

When you see the value of something---when you truly understand its
worth---you hold onto it. A person who truly sees the value of their marriage doesn't walk away
when things get hard. A person who truly sees the value of their health doesn't neglect their
body. A person who truly sees the value of their relationship with God
doesn't drift away when faith gets difficult. And a person who truly sees the church won't abandon it.

Not because the church is perfect. But because they see what Jesus is
building. Not because Christians never disappoint. But because they understand the
value of the body. Not because church is always easy. But because they've seen something
they can't unsee. THE PROGRESSION OF REVELATION
Let me show you how this works:
Stage 1: No revelation
"I love Jesus, but I don't need the church. I can worship God on my
own.

The church is full of hypocrites."
At this stage, you haven't yet seen the church the way Jesus sees her. Stage 2: Partial revelation
"I know the church is important. I should probably go more often. I see
the value of community."
At this stage, you're starting to see, but you haven't fully grasped
it yet. Stage 3: Full revelation
"I love the church because Jesus loves the church. I'm committed to
what He's building.

I can't imagine doing life without His body."
At this stage, you've seen it. And once you see it, you can't unsee it. IT'S NOT ABOUT FEELINGS
Now let me clarify something important:
This isn't about warm, fuzzy feelings. You're not going to wake up every Sunday morning excited about going to
church. Some days will be hard. Some Sundays you'll want to stay in
bed. That's not what I'm talking about. I'm talking about a deep, settled conviction that the church matters.

A commitment that isn't based on feelings but on revelation. A love that persists even when people disappoint you. Because here's the truth:
The church will disappoint you. People will let you down. Leaders will
fail. Things will go wrong. But none of that changes what you've seen. You've seen that Jesus loves the church. You've seen that He gave His life for her. You've seen that He's building something eternal.

And you can't unsee that. FALLING IN LOVE VS. STAYING IN LOVE
Let me use marriage as an analogy. When you first fall in love, everything is easy. The feelings are
strong. The excitement is real. You can't imagine ever not feeling this
way. But then life happens. Conflict arises. Disappointments come. The feelings fade. And that's when you have to choose: Will you stay in love? Staying in love isn't about feelings.

It's about commitment. It's
about choosing to love even when you don't feel like it. The same is true with the church. You might have had a moment when you fell in love with the church---when
you saw what Jesus was building and your heart leaped. But then life happens. Someone hurts you. A leader disappoints you. A decision frustrates you. And that's when you have to choose: Will you stay in love? Not because you always feel like it.

But because you've seen something
you can't unsee. You've seen that Jesus loves her. And that's enough. THE ALTERNATIVE
Let me show you what happens when you don't fall in love with the
church:
You fall away. Not necessarily from Jesus. But from His purposes. From His mission. From His body. You become a Christian in name but not in practice. You believe in Jesus but you're disconnected from His people. You have status but no power.

And eventually, your faith becomes cold. Because here's the reality:
You can't stay connected to the head while disconnecting from the
body. Jesus and the church are one. And when you reject the church, you're rejecting part of what Jesus is
building. THE HEART TRANSFORMATION
So how do you fall in love with the church? You ask God to give you His heart. You pray the prayer Paul prayed:
Ephesians 1:18 -- *I pray that the eyes of your heart may be
enlightened in order that you may know the hope to which he has called
you, the riches of his glorious inheritance in his holy people.*
Ask God to open your eyes.

Ask Him to show you what He sees when He looks at the church. Ask Him to give you His heart for His people. Because it's not something you can manufacture. It's something He
has to give you. THE TRANSFORMATION THAT HAPPENS
And when God opens your eyes, here's what changes:
1. You stop criticizing and start building
Instead of pointing out flaws, you become part of the solution.
2. You stop attending and start investing
Instead of being a consumer, you become a contributor.
3.

You stop complaining and start serving
Instead of demanding that the church meet your needs, you start meeting
the needs of others.
4. You stop isolating and start connecting
Instead of keeping people at arm's length, you open your heart to
community.
5. You stop leaving and start staying
Instead of giving up when things get hard, you commit for the long haul. When you love the church, you love Jesus.

When you serve the church, you serve Jesus. When you invest in the church, you invest in what Jesus is building. And that's when everything changes. THE FINAL WORD
So let me leave you with this:
You either fall in love or fall away. There's no middle ground. If you have a revelation of Jesus, you'll love the church. If you don't love the church, you don't yet have the full revelation. So stop making excuses.

Stop criticizing. Stop disconnecting. And start falling in love with what Jesus died to create. Because when you do, you'll never be the same. Your faith will come alive. Your purpose will become clear. Your joy will be full. Because you're aligned with the heart of Jesus. And His heart beats for the church. In the next chapter, we're going to get real about something: the
church isn't perfect. In fact, it's full of problems, disappointments,
and imperfect people.

So how do you stay committed when things fall
apart? How do you love something that's so flawed? Let me show you how
to deal with Bridezilla.*

chapters.title: Chapter 14: Bridezilla (The Church Isn't Perfect)
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about my wedding day. Jane was absolutely beautiful. Radiant. Stunning. The most gorgeous
bride I'd ever seen. Everything was perfect. Well\... almost everything. There were some hiccups. Some things didn't go exactly as planned. Some
moments of stress behind the scenes. But standing at that altar, watching Jane walk down the aisle, all I
could see was the woman I loved. Not the imperfections.

Not the stress. Not the things that went wrong. I saw the bride. Now let me tell you about the church. The church is Jesus' bride. And one day, she's going to be absolutely
perfect---radiant, glorious, without spot or wrinkle. But we're not there yet. Right now? The church has problems. Issues. Imperfections. And if you're going to stay committed to the church, you need to learn
how to love an imperfect bride.

Let me show you how. THE REALITY CHECK
Let's just be honest about something:
The church isn't perfect. There are hypocrites in the church. There are people who say one thing
and do another. There are leaders who fall. There are members who
gossip. There are decisions that are made poorly. There are conflicts
that shouldn't happen. The church is messy. And if you're waiting for the perfect church before you commit, you're
going to be waiting forever.

Because the perfect church doesn't exist. Why? Because the church is made up of imperfect people. Including you. Including me. THE BRIDEZILLA REALITY
Here's something most people don't talk about:
Every bride---no matter how beautiful---has moments when she acts like
Bridezilla. The stress of planning a wedding. The pressure of the big day. The
emotions running high. And suddenly, the beautiful bride becomes demanding, difficult, and
irrational.

Does that mean you stop loving her? Does that mean you cancel the
wedding? No. You recognize that she's stressed. You give her grace. You love her
through the difficult moments. Because you're committed to her, not to having a perfect
experience. The same is true with the church. There will be moments when the church acts like Bridezilla. Moments when things get messy. Moments when people disappoint you. Moments when you wonder if it's worth it.

But that doesn't change your commitment. Because you're committed to what Jesus is building, not to having a
perfect experience. THE THREE CATEGORIES OF PROBLEMS
Let me break down the problems you'll encounter in the church into
three categories:
Category 1: People Problems
This is when individuals in the church hurt you, disappoint you, or let
you down. Someone gossips about you. Someone criticizes you unfairly.

Someone
breaks a promise. Reality check: People are imperfect. And imperfect people will
disappoint you. But here's what you need to understand: The church didn't hurt you. A person did. And one person's failure doesn't negate the value of the whole body. Category 2: Leadership Problems
This is when pastors or leaders make poor decisions, fall into sin, or
fail to lead well. A pastor has a moral failure. A leader makes a bad financial decision.

The church goes in a direction you don't agree with. Reality check: Leaders are human. And humans make mistakes. But here's what you need to understand: Jesus is still the head of
the church, even when human leaders fail. Category 3: Systemic Problems
This is when the church as an institution has issues. Bureaucracy that slows things down. Traditions that need to change. Methods that aren't working anymore.

Reality check: Institutions aren't perfect. And the church is
constantly in need of reform. But here's what you need to understand: Jesus is still building His
church, even when the systems are broken. HOW TO STAY COMMITTED WHEN THINGS FALL APART
So how do you stay committed to an imperfect church? Here are five practical steps:
1. Remember what you're committed to
You're not committed to perfect people.

You're not committed to
flawless leadership. You're not committed to a pristine institution. You're committed to Jesus and what He's building. When people disappoint you, remember: Jesus never will.
2. Give grace like you've received grace
You're not perfect either. You've made mistakes. You've disappointed
people. You've needed grace. So give the same grace you've received. Colossians 3:13 -- *Bear with each other and forgive one another if
any of you has a grievance against someone.

Forgive as the Lord forgave
you.*
3. Be part of the solution, not part of the problem
It's easy to criticize. It's easy to point out flaws. It's easy to
complain. But what are you doing to make things better? Instead of criticizing the worship, join the worship team. Instead of
complaining about the sermons, pray for your pastor. Instead of
gossiping about problems, offer solutions. Stop being a critic and start being a builder.
4.

Keep the main thing the main thing
The main thing is Jesus. The main thing is the gospel. The main thing is
people coming to know Christ. Don't let secondary issues distract you from the primary mission.
5. Remember: Jesus is still working on the church
Ephesians 5:26-27 -- *\...to make her holy, cleansing her by the
washing with water through the word, and to present her to himself as a
radiant church, without stain or wrinkle or any other blemish, but holy
and blameless.*
Jesus isn't done yet.

The church isn't finished. He's still washing, still cleansing, still preparing the bride. And if Jesus hasn't given up on the church, neither should you. THE DANGER OF CHURCH SHOPPING
Now let me address something that's become epidemic:
Church shopping. "I'll try this church for a while. If I don't like it, I'll move to
another one. If that doesn't work out, I'll find a third one."
Here's the problem with that approach:
You'll never find the perfect church.

Because the perfect church doesn't exist. And even if you found a church that seemed perfect, the moment you
joined it, it would no longer be perfect. Because you're not perfect. So stop shopping for the perfect church. Start investing in an imperfect church. Commit. Serve. Give. Build. Because the church doesn't become better by people leaving. The church becomes better by people staying and helping. WHAT ABOUT WHEN YOU REALLY NEED TO LEAVE?

Now let me be clear:
There are times when leaving a church is the right decision. If a church is teaching false doctrine. If a church is abusive or
manipulative. If a church is engaging in immoral behavior. In those cases, you should leave. But here's what I see happening too often:
People leave because the music isn't their style. People leave because
they didn't like a sermon. People leave because someone hurt their
feelings.

Those aren't reasons to leave. Those are reasons to grow up. The church isn't a restaurant where you order exactly what you want. The church is a family. And families work through problems. THE COMMITMENT THAT CHANGES EVERYTHING
Let me tell you what happens when you commit to an imperfect church:
1. You grow spiritually
Because dealing with imperfect people forces you to develop patience,
grace, and humility.
2.

You discover your gifts
Because an imperfect church needs you to step up and serve.
3. You build deep relationships
Because real relationships are forged in the fire of working through
problems together.
4. You become part of something bigger than yourself
Because you're not just consuming---you're contributing to what Jesus
is building.
5. You experience the power of grace
Because you learn what it means to give grace and receive grace in
community.

JESUS LOVES BRIDEZILLA
Here's the most important thing I want you to understand:
Jesus loves the church---even when she acts like Bridezilla. He doesn't love her because she's perfect. He loves her because she's
His. And He's committed to her---not just when things are good, but when
things are messy. Ephesians 5:25 -- *Husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved
the church and gave himself up for her.*
"Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her."
Not when she was perfect.

Not when she had it all together. He loved her and gave Himself up for her while she was still broken,
still struggling, still imperfect. And if Jesus can love an imperfect church, so can you. THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge to you:
Stop waiting for the perfect church. Stop church shopping. Stop leaving every time something goes wrong. And start committing to an imperfect church. Because here's the truth:
The church needs you---flaws and all.

And you need the church---flaws and all. We're all imperfect people in an imperfect church, being made perfect
by a perfect Savior. And that's the beauty of it. So stop running from Bridezilla. And start loving her the way Jesus does. Because one day, she's going to be radiant, glorious, and perfect. But until then, we're all works in progress. And Jesus loves us anyway. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about the death of spiritual
individualism.

Because one of the biggest lies in modern Christianity is
the idea that "you are the church." Let me show you why that's not
true---and why it matters more than you think.*

chapters.title: Chapter 15: You're Not The Church (And That's The Point)
paragraphs:
Let me correct something that's become popular in Christian culture:
"You are the church!"
I hear this all the time. Well-meaning Christians trying to encourage
people that the church isn't a building, it's people. And they're half right. The church is people. But here's what they get wrong:
You are not the church. WE are the church. The difference matters more than you think. Let me explain. THE SHIFT FROM "I" TO "WE"
Modern Christianity has become dangerously individualistic.

"My personal relationship with Jesus." "My walk with God."
"My spiritual journey."
It's all about ME. And while there's truth to the fact that you have a personal
relationship with Jesus, the Bible paints a much bigger picture:
It's not just about you. It's about US.
1 Corinthians 12:27 -- *Now YOU (plural) are the body of Christ, and
each one of you is a part of it.*
Not "you (singular) are the body."
"YOU (plural) are the body."
The church is a collective.

A community. A body made up of many parts. You're a part. But you're not the whole. WHY "YOU ARE THE CHURCH" IS DANGEROUS
When you say "I am the church," you're implying several things that
aren't true:
1. You don't need anyone else
If you're the church all by yourself, then gathering with other
believers is optional. You can stay home, watch a sermon online, and call it church. But that's not how it works.
2.

You can decide what's right for you
If you're the church, then you get to define what church looks like for
you. You get to decide when you show up, how you participate, and what you
believe. But that's not how it works.
3. You're self-sufficient
If you're the church, then you don't need the gifts of others. You
don't need the body. You don't need community. But that's not how it works. The truth is: You need the rest of us.

And we need you. THE BODY ANALOGY
Paul uses the analogy of a body to explain the church for a reason:
1 Corinthians 12:14-21 -- *Even so the body is not made up of one
part but of many. Now if the foot should say, "Because I am not a hand,
I do not belong to the body," it would not for that reason stop being
part of the body. And if the ear should say, "Because I am not an eye,
I do not belong to the body," it would not for that reason stop being
part of the body.

If the whole body were an eye, where would the sense
of hearing be? If the whole body were an ear, where would the sense of
smell be? But in fact God has placed the parts in the body, every one of
them, just as he wanted them to be. If they were all one part, where
would the body be? As it is, there are many parts, but one body. The eye
cannot say to the hand, "I don't need you!" And the head cannot say
to the feet, "I don't need you!"*
Read that carefully.

You're a part. Not the whole. The foot can't say, "I'm the body." The hand can't say, "I'm the
body." The eye can't say, "I'm the body."
They're all PART of the body. And the body only functions when all the parts work together. WHAT YOU LOSE WHEN YOU GO SOLO
So what do you lose when you try to be "the church" by yourself?
1. You lose perspective
When you're isolated, you only see things from your viewpoint.

You miss
the wisdom, insight, and perspective that come from other believers. Proverbs 11:14 -- *For lack of guidance a nation falls, but victory
is won through many advisers.*
2. You lose accountability
When you're alone, there's no one to challenge you, correct you, or
call you out when you're drifting. Hebrews 3:13 -- *But encourage one another daily, as long as it is
called "Today," so that none of you may be hardened by sin's
deceitfulness.*
3.

You lose your gifts
Your gifts aren't meant to be used in isolation. They're meant to
serve the body. When you're disconnected, your gifts stay dormant.
4. You lose the power of unity
There's a power that's released when believers agree, gather, and move
together. Matthew 18:19 -- *"Again, truly I tell you that if two of you on
earth agree about anything they ask for, it will be done for them by my
Father in heaven."*
When you go solo, you lose that power.
5.

You lose the joy of community
God created us for relationship. For connection. For belonging. When you isolate yourself, you miss out on the joy that comes from doing
life together. THE DEATH OF SPIRITUAL INDIVIDUALISM
Here's what I'm asking you to do:
Kill your spiritual individualism. Stop thinking of yourself as a solo Christian who can make it on your
own. Stop saying, "I don't need anyone else."
Stop acting like the church is optional.

Because the truth is: You need us. And we need you. Romans 12:4-5 -- *For just as each of us has one body with many
members, and these members do not all have the same function, so in
Christ we, though many, form one body, and each member belongs to all
the others.*
"Each member belongs to all the others."
You don't just belong to Jesus. You belong to the body. You're not an independent contractor. You're part of a family.

THE SHIFT IN IDENTITY
Let me show you what happens when you make the shift from "I" to
"WE":
Before: "I am a Christian." After: "I am part of the body of
Christ."
Before: "My relationship with Jesus." After: "Our relationship
with Jesus."
Before: "What can the church do for me?" After: "What can I do
for the church?"
Before: "I'll participate when it's convenient." After: "I'm
committed because I'm part of something bigger."
This shift in identity changes everything.

You stop being a consumer and become a contributor. You stop being a spectator and become a participant. You stop being isolated and become connected. THE BIBLICAL PATTERN
Look at how the early church operated:
Acts 2:44-45 -- *All the believers were together and had everything
in common. They sold property and possessions to give to anyone who had
need.*
"All the believers were together."
Not scattered.

Not isolated. Together. Acts 4:32 -- *All the believers were one in heart and mind. No one
claimed that any of their possessions was their own, but they shared
everything they had.*
"All the believers were one."
Not independent. Not autonomous. One. This is the biblical pattern. Not "I am the church."
"WE are the church."
WHY THIS MATTERS
So why does this distinction matter? Because individualism is killing the church.

When everyone thinks they can do Christianity on their own:
•  Churches become weak
•  Community breaks down
•  Mission gets neglected
•  The body becomes fragmented
But when we embrace the "WE" identity:
•  Churches become strong
•  Community flourishes
•  Mission gets accomplished
•  The body functions as it should
You're not the church. But you're part of something beautiful when
you join with US.

THE CHALLENGE
So here's my challenge to you:
Stop trying to be the church by yourself. Stop thinking you're self-sufficient. Stop isolating yourself from the body. And embrace the "WE" identity. You're part of the body of Christ. You need the rest of us. And we need you. Not because any of us are perfect. But because God designed it this way. He created the church to be a body---many parts working together for one
purpose.

And when we function as a body, we become what Jesus intended:
A force that storms the gates of hell. A light that shines in the
darkness. A bride that reflects the glory of the groom. You're not the church. But WE are. And that's exactly the point. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about the revelation that
changes everything. Because there's a difference between knowing about
the church and truly seeing what the church is.

And when God opens your
eyes to see it, you'll never be the same.*

chapters.title: Chapter 16: The Revelation That Changes Everything
paragraphs:
Let me tell you about the difference between knowing and seeing. You can know something intellectually without ever truly seeing
it. You can know that the church is important---but never see what Jesus is
building. You can know that you should be committed---but never see why it
matters. You can know all the right theology---but never have the revelation that
changes everything. There's a difference between information and revelation.

And when God gives you revelation about the church, everything changes. Let me show you what I mean. PETER'S REVELATION
Matthew 16:13-18 -- *When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea
Philippi, he asked his disciples, "Who do people say the Son of Man
is?" They replied, "Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and
still others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets." "But what about you?"
he asked. "Who do you say I am?" Simon Peter answered, "You are the
Messiah, the Son of the living God." Jesus replied, "Blessed are you,
Simon son of Jonah, for this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood,
but by my Father in heaven.

And I tell you that you are Peter, and on
this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not
overcome it."*
Look at what Jesus said to Peter:
"This was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by my Father in
heaven."
Peter didn't figure it out. He didn't deduce it. He didn't reason his
way to the answer. God revealed it to him. And the moment Peter received that revelation---the moment he truly SAW
who Jesus was---Jesus immediately started talking about the church.

Why? Because you can't have a full revelation of Jesus without seeing His
heart for the church. The two are inseparable. THE MYSTERY THAT REQUIRES REVELATION
Remember what we learned in Chapter 5? The church is a musterion---a mystery that can only be known by
divine revelation. Ephesians 3:3-5 -- *That is, the mystery made known to me by
revelation, as I have already written briefly. In reading this, then,
you will be able to understand my insight into the mystery of Christ,
which was not made known to people in other generations as it has now
been revealed by the Spirit to God's holy apostles and prophets.*
The church wasn't something people figured out.

It wasn't a good idea that evolved over time. It was a mystery that God revealed. And you need that same revelation today. Not just information about the church. Not just knowledge about
ecclesiology. You need God to open your eyes to see what the church really is. WHAT HAPPENS WHEN GOD OPENS YOUR EYES
Let me tell you what happens when God gives you revelation about the
church:
1. You can't unsee it
Once you see what Jesus is building, you can't go back to seeing the
church as optional or insignificant.

It's like those optical illusions---once you see the hidden image, you
can't unsee it. It's always there.
2. Everything else shifts into perspective
Your priorities change. Your commitments change. Your schedule changes. Because you've seen what matters most.
3. Criticism turns to compassion
Instead of standing outside pointing at flaws, you get inside and become
part of the solution. Because you see what Jesus sees---a bride He's preparing, not a broken
institution.
4.

Excuses disappear
You stop making excuses for why you can't commit, can't serve, can't
give. Because you've seen something worth giving your life to.
5. Passion replaces duty
Church stops being something you "should" do and becomes something you
"want" to do. Not because you're perfect. But because you've seen what Jesus is
building. THE PRAYER FOR REVELATION
So how do you get this revelation? You ask for it.

Ephesians 1:17-18 -- *I keep asking that the God of our Lord Jesus
Christ, the glorious Father, may give you the Spirit of wisdom and
revelation, so that you may know him better. I pray that the eyes of
your heart may be enlightened in order that you may know the hope to
which he has called you, the riches of his glorious inheritance in his
holy people.*
Paul prayed for believers to receive revelation.

And you can pray the same prayer:
"God, give me the Spirit of wisdom and revelation. Open the eyes of
my heart. Show me what You see when You look at the church. Help me see
what Jesus is building. Give me Your heart for Your people."
This isn't a one-time prayer. It's an ongoing request. Because the more you see, the more you want to see. THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HEAD KNOWLEDGE AND HEART REVELATION
Let me show you the difference:
Head knowledge says: "I know the church is important."
Heart revelation says: "I can't imagine doing life without the
church."
Head knowledge says: "I should probably go to church more often."
Heart revelation says: "I'm committed to the church because I've
seen what Jesus is building."
Head knowledge says: "The Bible says the church matters."
Heart revelation says: "I've encountered Jesus in His body, and
I'm forever changed."
Head knowledge can be argued with.

Heart revelation can't. When you've truly seen something, no one can convince you that you
didn't see it. MY STORY OF REVELATION
Let me tell you about my revelation moment. I've already shared about the encounter I had with Jesus---the download
of 4 trillion gigabytes of information. But let me tell you what specifically happened regarding the church. Before that moment, I attended church. I believed church was important.

I knew the theology. But I didn't have the revelation. Then, in that encounter with Jesus, something shifted. It wasn't just that I learned more about the church. I received His
heart for the church. Suddenly, I didn't just attend church---I loved the church. I didn't just believe it was important---I saw it as the most important
thing on earth. I didn't just participate---I gave my life to it. Because I had seen what Jesus is building.

And once you see it, you can't unsee it. THE REVELATION PRODUCES COMMITMENT
Here's what I've noticed:
People who have revelation about the church don't struggle with
commitment. They don't need to be convinced to show up. They don't need to be
guilted into serving. They don't need to be persuaded to give. Because they've seen something. And what they've seen is so valuable, so significant, so eternal, that
everything else pales in comparison.

Commitment flows naturally from revelation. When you see what Jesus is building, you want to be part of it. When you understand the value of the body, you can't imagine being
disconnected. When you grasp the mission of the church, you give your life to it. Not out of obligation. Out of revelation. THE DIFFERENCE REVELATION MAKES
Let me show you what changes when you have revelation:
Without revelation:
•  Church is a place you go
•  Serving is something you should do
•  Giving is a duty
•  Community is optional
•  The church is one of many priorities
With revelation:
•  Church is a family you belong to
•  Serving is a privilege
•  Giving is a joy
•  Community is essential
•  The church is THE priority (after Jesus)
Everything changes when you see what Jesus sees.

YOU'LL NEVER ABANDON WHAT YOU TRULY SEE
Let me say it again:
You'll never abandon what you truly see. When you have a revelation of the church---when you truly see what Jesus
is building---you won't walk away. Not when people disappoint you. Not when leaders fail. Not when things
get hard. Because you've seen something too valuable to abandon. This is why some people stay committed to the church for decades, while
others leave at the first sign of trouble.

The difference isn't personality. The difference is revelation. Those who stay have seen something. Those who leave haven't seen it yet. THE INVITATION
So here's what I'm asking you to do:
Ask God for revelation. Stop settling for head knowledge. Stop being satisfied with just knowing that the church is important. Ask God to open your eyes. Ask Him to show you what Jesus is building. Ask Him to give you His heart for His people.

Ask Him for the revelation that changes everything. Because once you see it, you'll never be the same. Your commitment will be unshakable. Your passion will be unstoppable. Your purpose will be clear. Because you'll have seen what Jesus sees. And you can't unsee that. In the next chapter, we're going to talk about where to find Jesus on
Sunday morning. Because if you want to encounter Jesus in a way that
goes beyond your personal quiet time, there's a specific place He
promised to be.
Let me show you where that is.*

chapters.title: Chapter 17: Where To Find Jesus On Sunday Morning
paragraphs:
Let me ask you a question:
Where do you go to find Jesus? Your quiet time? Your prayer closet? Your worship playlist? Your
journal? A walk in nature? Those are all good answers. You can encounter Jesus in all of those
places. But let me tell you where Jesus said He would be:
Matthew 18:20 -- *"For where two or three gather in my name, there
am I with them."*
Not "where one sits alone." Not "where someone watches online." Not
"where an individual reads their Bible."
"Where two or three gather."
If you want to find Jesus on Sunday morning, He's in the gathering.

He's in the church. Let me show you what I mean. THE INCARNATION PRINCIPLE
Here's something most Christians miss:
Jesus still wants a body. When Jesus was on earth, He had a physical body. He used that body to
heal, to teach, to serve, to love. But then He ascended to heaven. And His physical body left earth. So what did He do? He created a new body---the church.
1 Corinthians 12:27 -- *Now you are the body of Christ, and each one
of you is a part of it.*
Colossians 1:24 -- \...his body, which is the church.

The church is not a metaphor. It's not just a nice illustration. The church is literally the body of Christ on earth today. When Jesus left physically, He didn't leave us orphans. He gave us His
body---the church---so that He could still be physically present in the
world. That's the incarnation principle. God became flesh in Jesus. And Jesus continues to be incarnate in the
church. WHERE JESUS PROMISED TO BE
Jesus made a lot of promises.

But one of the most significant is this:
Matthew 18:20 -- *"For where two or three gather in my name, there
am I with them."*
"THERE AM I WITH THEM."
Not "I might be." Not "I'll think about being."
"I AM with them."
This is a promise. A guarantee. A certainty. When believers gather in Jesus' name, He is present. Not symbolically. Not spiritually in a distant way. Tangibly. Really. Powerfully. If you want to find Jesus, go where He promised to be.

THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN ALONE AND TOGETHER
Now let me be clear:
Jesus is with you when you pray alone. He's with you when you read your
Bible by yourself. He's with you in your personal worship. But there's a dimension of His presence that's only accessible in
the gathering. Think about it like this:
When I'm with Jane, just the two of us, it's beautiful. Intimate. Personal. But when our whole family gathers---kids, grandkids, everyone
together---there's a different dynamic.

A different joy. A different
kind of presence. Both are good. But they're different. The same is true with Jesus. Your personal time with Him is essential. Beautiful. Necessary. But there's something that happens when the church gathers that
doesn't happen when you're alone. A manifestation of His presence that's unique to the corporate
gathering. WHAT HAPPENS WHEN THE CHURCH GATHERS
So what happens when believers gather that doesn't happen in isolation?
1.

Heaven invades earth
Matthew 6:10 -- *"Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as
it is in heaven."*
When the church gathers, we're not just having a nice meeting. We're
creating a space where heaven touches earth.
2. Jesus shows up in power
Acts 4:31 -- *After they prayed, the place where they were meeting
was shaken. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the
word of God boldly.*
Notice: "After they prayed"---together---"the place was
shaken."
Not a quiet, private moment.

A shaking. A manifestation of power.
3. Spiritual gifts operate
1 Corinthians 14:26 -- *What then shall we say, brothers and
sisters? When you come together, each of you has a hymn, or a word of
instruction, a revelation, a tongue or an interpretation. Everything
must be done so that the church may be built up.*
Prophecy flows. Teaching happens. Encouragement is given. Healing
occurs. These gifts are designed to operate in the gathering.
4.

Faith multiplies
Hebrews 10:25 -- *Not giving up meeting together, as some are in the
habit of doing, but encouraging one another---and all the more as you
see the Day approaching.*
When you gather with other believers, their faith strengthens yours. And
your faith strengthens theirs. There's a multiplication effect in the gathering.
5. Unity is displayed
John 17:21 -- *"May they all be one, Father, just as you are in me
and I am in you.

May they also be in us so that the world may believe
that you have sent me."*
Unity is visible. And unity is powerful. When the world sees believers gathered in unity, it's a testimony to
the reality of Jesus. YOU'RE NOT JUST ATTENDING---YOU'RE ENCOUNTERING
Here's what I need you to understand:
When you come to church, you're not just attending a service. You're encountering Jesus in His body. You're meeting with the living God.

You're participating in something heaven is watching. This isn't casual. This isn't optional. This is sacred. Hebrews 12:22-23 -- *But you have come to Mount Zion, to the city of
the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem. You have come to thousands upon
thousands of angels in joyful assembly, to the church of the firstborn,
whose names are written in heaven.*
When the church gathers, you're joining a cosmic assembly.

Angels are present. Heaven is paying attention. This is a big deal. THE POWER OF CORPORATE WORSHIP
Let me tell you about corporate worship. When I worship alone, it's beautiful. I encounter God. I lift my hands. I sing to Him. But when the church worships together, something different happens. There's an amplification. A multiplication. A breakthrough. Psalm 22:3 -- *Yet you are enthroned as the Holy One; you are the
one Israel praises.*
God is enthroned on the praises of His people.

Not just your individual praise. The corporate praise of His people. When hundreds or thousands of voices unite in worship, God's presence
manifests in a unique way. That's why you need corporate worship. Not as a replacement for personal worship. But as a dimension of worship
you can't access alone. THE COST OF STAYING HOME
So what do you lose when you stay home?
1. You miss the manifestation of Jesus' presence in the gathering
He's with you at home.

But there's a dimension of His presence that's
only accessible when believers gather.
2. You miss the operation of spiritual gifts
Gifts that are meant to build you up, encourage you, and equip you.
3. You miss the multiplication of faith
Your faith stays the same when you're isolated. But it multiplies when
you're connected.
4. You miss the joy of corporate worship
There's a joy in worshiping with the body that you can't experience
alone.
5.

You miss being part of what Jesus is building
When you're disconnected, you're a spectator. When you're gathered,
you're a participant. ONLINE ISN'T THE SAME
Now let me address something:
Online church isn't the same as in-person gathering. I'm grateful for technology. I'm thankful for live streams. They're a
great resource for people who are sick, traveling, or unable to attend. But they're not a replacement for gathering.

Here's why:
1. You can't experience the fullness of community through a screen
Real relationships require physical presence.
2. You can't participate in the gathering when you're watching
You're a consumer, not a contributor.
3. You miss the power of corporate presence
There's something that happens when bodies are in the same room that
doesn't happen through a screen. Online church is better than nothing.

But it's not the same as
gathering. THE INVITATION
So here's what I'm asking you to do:
Show up. Not just physically, but with expectation. Come expecting to encounter Jesus. Come expecting to experience His presence. Come expecting to be part of something bigger than yourself. Because Jesus promised to be there. Matthew 18:20 -- *"For where two or three gather in my name, there
am I with them."*
He's not there because the building is sacred.

He's not there because the worship is perfect. He's not there because the preaching is amazing. He's there because His people are gathered. And when you show up to the gathering, you're showing up to meet Jesus. WHERE TO FIND JESUS
So let me answer the question I started with:
Where do you go to find Jesus? You can find Him in your quiet time. You can find Him in your personal
worship. You can find Him in prayer.

But if you want to find Him in the fullness of His body, in the power
of corporate worship, in the manifestation of spiritual gifts, in the
unity of believers gathered together---
Go to church. Because that's where He promised to be. And Jesus keeps His promises. In the final chapter, we're going to talk about the fullness you've
been missing. Because there's a completeness, a wholeness, a
satisfaction that you'll never experience as long as you're
disconnected from the body.

Let me show you what you've been
missing---and how to find it.*

chapters.title: Chapter 18: The Fullness You've Been Missing
paragraphs:
We've come to the end of our journey together.
Eighteen chapters. Thousands of words. One central message:
Jesus and His church are one.
And if you've made it this far, my prayer is that God has opened your
eyes to see what you couldn't see before.
But let me end with this:
There's a fullness you've been missing.
A completeness. A wholeness. A satisfaction that your soul has been
craving.
And you'll never experience it as long as you're disconnected from the
body of Christ.
Let me show you what I mean---and why this matters more than you think.
THE FULLNESS OF CHRIST
Ephesians 1:22-23 -- *And God placed all things under his feet and
appointed him to be head over everything for the church, which is his
body, the fullness of him who fills everything in every way.*
Read that last phrase again. Slowly. Let it sink in.
"The church, which is his body, THE FULLNESS of him who fills
everything in every way."
Not "a part" of Christ's fullness. Not "a piece" of Christ's
fullness. Not "an aspect" of Christ's fullness.
THE FULLNESS.
The complete expression. The total manifestation. The whole package.
You can know Jesus personally. You can have a relationship with Him. You
can experience His love, His grace, His forgiveness.
But you can't experience the FULLNESS of Christ without being
connected to His body.
Because the fullness---all of it---is found in the body.
Think about that.
Everything you've been searching for. Everything you've been longing
for. Everything that's been missing in your spiritual life.
It's in the body.
WHAT YOU'RE MISSING WHEN YOU'RE DISCONNECTED
Let me be specific about what you lose when you try to follow Jesus
outside of His church:
1. You miss the fullness of His presence
Yes, Jesus is with you personally. But there's a dimension of His
presence---a manifestation, a power, a tangibility---that's only
accessible in the gathering of believers.
When two or three gather, Jesus shows up differently. More powerfully.
More completely.
You're missing that.
2. You miss the fullness of your purpose
Your gifts aren't meant to be used in isolation. Your calling isn't
meant to be fulfilled alone. Your purpose is found in the context of the
body.
Right now, your gifts are dormant. Your calling is unclear. Your purpose
feels fuzzy.
Because you're disconnected from the place where they come alive.
3. You miss the fullness of community
God created you for relationship. For connection. For deep, authentic,
life-giving belonging.
And you'll never experience that depth outside of the church.
You can have friends. You can have family. You can have coworkers.
But you'll never have what the body offers---people who see you, know
you, love you, and are committed to you for the long haul.
4. You miss the fullness of spiritual growth
You need other believers to sharpen you, challenge you, encourage you,
and correct you.
When you're isolated, your growth plateaus. Your perspective narrows.
Your blind spots multiply.
The body was designed to grow you in ways you could never grow
alone.
5. You miss the fullness of impact
You can make a difference alone. You can do good things. You can help
people.
But you can't change the world alone.
The church has a collective impact that no individual could ever
achieve. When the body moves together, hell trembles.
And you're missing that power.
6. You miss the fullness of joy
There's a joy that comes from being part of something bigger than
yourself. A joy that comes from giving your life to what Jesus is
building.
It's the joy of purpose. The joy of belonging. The joy of contribution.
And you'll never taste it outside the church.
THE INCOMPLETENESS OF ISOLATION
Here's what I've learned over decades of ministry:
You can be a Christian and still feel incomplete.
You can be saved. Filled with the Spirit. Walking with God daily.
And still feel like something's missing.
Like there's a hole you can't fill. A longing you can't name. A
restlessness you can't shake.
And often---more often than you realize---what's missing is
connection to the body.
Because God didn't design you to do this alone.
He didn't save you just so you could have a personal relationship with
Him and live in isolation.
He saved you to be part of a body. A family. A mission that's bigger
than you.
And when you're disconnected from that body, you feel the absence.
Not because Jesus isn't enough. But because you're not experiencing
the fullness of what He designed for you.
You're living on crumbs when there's a feast waiting.
THE PSALM 23 PRINCIPLE
Psalm 23:1 -- The LORD is my shepherd, I lack nothing.
When God is your shepherd, you lack nothing.
But here's what most people miss:
You lack nothing when you're IN THE FLOCK.
A sheep separated from the flock is vulnerable. Isolated. Exposed. In
danger.
It might survive for a while. It might even convince itself it's better
off alone.
But it lacks what only the flock can provide: protection, provision,
and belonging.
The same is true for you.
When you're disconnected from the church, you lack something.
Not because God isn't your shepherd. But because you've separated
yourself from the flock He placed you in.
And that lack---that incompleteness---will gnaw at you until you come
home.
THE LONGING YOU CAN'T NAME
Maybe you've felt it.
A longing. A restlessness. A sense that something's off.
You pray more. You read your Bible more. You try to get closer to God.
But the longing remains.
Let me tell you what that longing is:
You were created for community.
You were designed to be part of a body.
You were made to belong to something bigger than yourself.
And that longing won't go away until you connect to the church.
Not just attend. Connect.
Not just show up. Belong.
Not just participate. Commit.
Because the fullness you're longing for is found in the body.
WHAT FULLNESS LOOKS LIKE
So what does fullness actually look like?
Let me paint you a picture:
Fullness is:
•  Worshiping with your brothers and sisters and feeling heaven invade
earth
•  Serving alongside people who've become more than friends---they're
family
•  Using your gifts to build something eternal and seeing lives changed
•  Being known, loved, challenged, and sharpened by real community
•  Finding your purpose in the context of the mission and never looking
back
•  Experiencing breakthrough that only happens when believers gather
and agree
•  Belonging to something so much bigger than yourself that it takes
your breath away
•  Being part of what Jesus is building and knowing you're exactly
where you're supposed to be
That's fullness.
Not perfection. Not a problem-free existence. Not a life without
challenges.
But completeness. Wholeness. Satisfaction at the soul level.
The sense that you're living the life you were created for.
The joy of being part of God's family.
The power of being connected to Christ's body.
That's what you've been missing.
THE INVITATION ONE FINAL TIME
So let me invite you one last time:
Come home to the church.
If you've been disconnected, reconnect.
If you've been hurt, find healing in a healthy community.
If you've been critical, become a builder.
If you've been isolated, open your heart to belonging.
If you've been skeptical, ask God to open your eyes.
Because the fullness you've been missing is waiting for you in the
body of Christ.
Not in a perfect church. Those don't exist.
But in an imperfect church full of imperfect people being perfected by a
perfect Savior.
That's where you belong.
And until you're connected there---truly connected, not just
attending---you'll keep feeling that incompleteness.
That restlessness.
That longing for something more.
What you're longing for is the fullness of Christ found in His
body.
THE COST OF STAYING DISCONNECTED
Let me be direct with you one more time:
Every day you stay disconnected is a day you live incomplete.
Every Sunday you skip is a Sunday you miss experiencing Jesus in the
fullness of His body.
Every week you isolate yourself is a week your gifts stay dormant, your
purpose stays unclear, and your impact stays limited.
You're not just missing out. You're starving yourself.
You're choosing crumbs when there's a feast.
You're choosing isolation when there's belonging.
You're choosing incompleteness when there's fullness.
Why?
Because you've been hurt? The body can heal you.
Because you've been disappointed? The body can restore you.
Because you don't think you need it? You're wrong. You do.
MY PRAYER FOR YOU
Let me close with a prayer---not just a nice ending, but a heartfelt
plea:
Father, I pray for every person reading these words right now.
*If they've been disconnected from Your church, draw them back with
cords of love.*
*If they've been hurt, pour out Your healing and lead them to a healthy
community.*
*If they've been critical, soften their hearts and help them see Your
bride through Your eyes.*
*If they've been isolated, break through their walls and show them
their desperate need for the body.*
Open their eyes to see what Jesus is building.
*Give them a revelation of the church that they can't unsee, can't
unhear, can't ignore.*
Help them fall in love with what You love.
*And bring them into the fullness---the complete, satisfying,
life-giving fullness---of what You've designed for them.*
Stop them from settling for less.
Stop them from living incomplete.
Stop them from trying to separate what You've joined together.
And bring them home to the family You created them to be part of.
In Jesus' mighty name, Amen.
THE FINAL WORD
Here's my final word to you:
Jesus and His church are one.
You cannot love Jesus without loving His bride.
You cannot follow Jesus without being part of His body.
You cannot experience the fullness of Christ without connecting to His
church.
Whom God has joined together, let no man separate.
I started this book by confronting a lie: "I love Jesus but not the
church."
And I've spent eighteen chapters showing you why that statement is not
only false---it's impossible.
Because when you truly see Jesus, you receive His heart. And His heart
beats for the church.
So stop trying to separate what God has joined together.
Stop standing outside criticizing what Jesus died to create.
Stop isolating yourself from the very thing that brings fullness.
And start falling in love with what Jesus loves most.
Because the church is:
•  The bride Jesus is coming back for
•  The body He's building
•  The delivery system of God's grace
•  The pillar and foundation of truth
•  The last organization standing
•  The hope of the world
•  Your family
And you're part of it.
Not because you're perfect. But because Jesus chose you.
Not because you deserve it. But because of grace.
Not because you have it all figured out. But because you're willing to
be part of what He's building.
Welcome home.
Welcome to the church---the most important, most powerful, most eternal
organization on planet earth.
This is where you belong.
This is where you'll find the fullness you've been missing.
This is where you'll discover your purpose, your people, and your
passion.
This is home.
So come in. Stay committed. Fall in love. Get connected. Serve with joy.
Give generously. Build faithfully.
Because Jesus and His church are one.
And when you embrace the church, you're embracing Him.
When you love the church, you're loving Him.
When you serve the church, you're serving Him.
Stop living incomplete.
Start experiencing fullness.
The church is waiting for you.
Jesus is waiting for you.
And the fullness you've been missing?
It's waiting too.
NOW WHAT?
If this book has impacted you, here's what I want you to do---not
tomorrow, not next week, but starting RIGHT NOW:
1. Find a healthy, Bible-believing, Jesus-exalting church
Not a perfect church---those don't exist. But a church that loves
Jesus, preaches the gospel, and is committed to making disciples. If you
don't know where to start, ask God to lead you. He will.
2. Get connected immediately
Don't just attend. Join a small group. Start serving. Build
relationships. Dive in. The deeper you go, the more fullness you'll
experience.
3. Make an unbreakable commitment
Decide right now that you're in. Not just when it's convenient. Not
just when you feel like it. But committed for the long haul. Through the
highs and the lows. Through the joys and the challenges.
4. Share this message with someone who needs it
If this book helped you see the church differently, give it to someone
who's disconnected. Recommend it to someone who's been hurt. Share it
with someone who needs to hear this truth. Be part of bringing others
home.
5. Pray for revelation daily
Ask God every single day to continue opening your eyes to see what He's
building. Pray for a deeper love for the church. Ask Him to give you His
heart for His people. This isn't a one-time prayer---it's a daily
conversation.
The journey doesn't end here.
This is just the beginning.
You've read the book. You've heard the truth. You've seen the vision.
Now it's time to live it.
Stop making excuses. Stop delaying. Stop waiting for the "right time."
The right time is now.
The fullness is waiting.
Come home.
